#🌐︱general-chat 19:30:33 dirvine.
Ach, the folk are still there. Some people have changed their opinion and whatever, but the people are still there, I think. There's folk still working on the project that were working on it all those years ago. The vision's not changed, the goal is still the same, but it's just natural. It's natural that—probably the closer we get to doing something quite transformative—the more aggressive and the more cutting the criticism will be. There will be folk that actually also feel as though they're betrayed or folk that feel they're not listened to, or there's all different sorts of things. It's just life. That's all. It's just life. We're doing the same project we were always doing. We're working on the same code base that we've always been working on. Just people see it very differently now. Don't know why, but they do. There's quite a lot of people repeatedly demanding answers — continually demanding that they be told whatever, or demanding that we do something different — or demanding that the team do x, while other folks demand the team do y. Even in the forum you can see people saying/arguing it should be x or arguing it should be y. The forum doesn't come to agreement very often. It's very unlikely that anybody from the team could get agreement in the forum. The old days were good. I enjoyed them. I enjoyed the spirit of "we're building something." There's nothing there yet—no money for folk to get their selves all twisted up about. But there is now. This is just the way it'll be from now on.

#🌐︱general-chat 20:02:06 dirvine.
This is the issue—and probably why there's confusion. It seems we don't have it. There's no proof we don't have it. There's an accusation that we don't have it. We don't really know what the accusation is, but we don't have something apparently. But the thing that I can guarantee that we don't have is time. The team are unbelievably busy and you can see even from your communication here this evening (nothing specific, a particular allegation not made by you, made by somebody else on another forum in some place that we don't know, in a thread that we don't know or whatever, but some accusation) and we have to hunt that down, go to that person, answer that person, and not do the work that we need to do to get the network running. So hopefully you can see where the issue is there: if we have to stop the team for every single accusation from every single corner, then that kills the network. And if I was a competitor to this network—I didn't want it to operate. I would be telling the team, "Go and respond to all these people." But I'm not a competitor. I do want it to succeed. And I'll be saying to the team: keep working, keep going forward. Don't stop.

📅 Wednesday, 2025-08-13
#🌐︱general-chat 07:14:24 dirvine.
Here is a wee utility called sb (saorsa browser). As we enter the agentic coding days with claude code, cursor-agent, openai cli, gemini-cli, qwen-cli etc. then you have these agents do code/docs etc. but often you don't see all they produce, so you get vscode/vim etc. to help you and then you are distracted (not so much with vim). So this wee utility is a cli and shows you your markdown or code files in preview mode (i.e. rendered) and also allows delete/move etc. as well. You can press "e" and edit any file in real time and so on. Think of this as a cli tool that allows you to quickly track the work, check the work and get to grips with the docs as your agents work away in the background. https://github.com/dirvine/sb/blob/main/README.md If you have rust installed then `cargo install sb` will do it for you. then in any dir just type `sb` and you have a view of your code and docs The intention is a new cli browser for a markdown only web as we move forward, but that is a side qwest for now.
❤️ 8
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 10:48:13 traktion0_20257 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
Here is a wee utility called sb (saorsa browser). As we enter the agentic coding days with claude co ... [truncated]
How have you find using AI with an evolving code base? I had an interesting chat with an AI enthusiast the other day. He was suggesting that AI struggled with an evolving codebase, as they couldn't learn about the new changes easily enough. I understand that writing whole apps from scratch can work well, especially with contained and easily/fully defined requirements. However, I'd be interested to hear you experience when working on shared code, especially when other humans are involved.
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 11:00:40 dirvine.
It's all about specification, design document, up-to-date documentation. The code has really little to do with it. You have to document everything, document clearly your expectation, do test plans, test-driven development, have agents that run your tests, agents that write your tests, agents that write your code, code review agents, documentation agents. So it's a much larger job than writing code. The code writing part of it is really quite tiny. If you imagine it's like managing a team of autistic savants in terms of the code, they'll do exactly what you ask them, given the context that you give them. If your code base is evolving and you're not evolving your documentation with it, then that's maybe something only humans can handle. But if you evolve your documentation along with the code base and have extremely detailed, thought-out plans for what you're going to do next—it works exceptionally well. Way, way, way, way better than a human.
❤️ 3
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 11:02:38 dirvine.
What I should say is: if anyone picks up an AI tool and just types prompts into it, they're going to have a terrible experience—unless they're just doing toy apps or the kind of apps you're describing (the kind of vibe coding type thing). For decent size code bases, it's all about becoming a manager of a team that will do exactly what you tell them.
👍 1
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 11:04:32 dirvine.
The side projects I'm working on, I would probably not let a human interfere with the code or interact with it because I don't think they would update the documentation and the tests as well as the AI does. So I think if you get humans interfering with that process, you have to put another process in place to then capture all the changes—which is fine because the AI can look at the Git log. If the human has put decent commit messages to explain the thought process, why they were doing the change, what they hoped to accomplish, and the results of the test to prove what they accomplished, that might be okay. But humans tend not to do that part, whereas I can make the AI do that part.

I am keen to try AI out on some smaller, stand alone, projects to start with and get a feel for it. Maybe it will be rewarding in a different sort of way, similar to going from low level coding, to using frameworks, etc. Less effort to achieve more can be fulfilling, as long as the engineering process is still stimulating.

That SB module, for instance—100% the code written by AI. The basic functionality was there in about 20 minutes. Took me about four hours to prepare the plan, the documentation, the testing I wanted. The code part itself was about 20 minutes—and that was all just done in the background.

great workflow, would help me immensely with my home pet project(s). At work we have a large codebase and using Visual Studio and I do not use AI at all. At home I use Visual Studio Code and try to use Copilot AI. But only like a "programming buddy" usually ask for "his" opinion after I write a function / piece of code. So it is more like a better compiler. But what you describe seems daunting but also very attractive. Although I am very strict for the quallity of my code (mainly performance). To have a team of AI "agents" somehow specialized and me working more like a manager. Can you point me to some tools (ideally opensource) / tutorials where I can start experimenting with that please ?
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 13:07:12 dirvine.
I use Claude code quite a bit, and I've got quite a significant setup there with various commands that I've written, as well as hooks and agents. I also use qwen-cli, Cursor Agent, as well as OpenAI CLI tool, so I use quite a mixture. I tend to rely heavily on tight specifications, design documents and different agents to write code, test code, check the security of code, write tests, run tests, etc. So they all work independently and they should check each other's code. On top of that, I'll run many of the different things like Qwen, OpenAi, and get them to evaluate how well the specification has been met by the other AI. So quite a bit of back and forth with various AIs. Unfortunately, I don't think there's really a shortcut. I think it comes down to what would be your management style—if you had very capable programmers, designers, testers, DevOps people that will do exactly what you say. That really reflects a lot in how well you write the specification. For instance, I also use MCP servers, so I've got a DigitalOcean MCP, a GitHub MCP, Docker MCP—so I can do things like: just tell my AI, "Set up Docker instances in five discrete geographies, start nodes on each of them, give me a latency report for those nodes and watch the cost." At the same time, we committed code to GitHub and telling the GitHub agent to watch the workflows, make sure everything's up to date, check the security analysis. If there's any issues with workflow: let's pull it down, write a specification for a fix, we'll agree the fix and then move forward. The Docker guy—sometimes I'm speaking to him—and creating a whole bunch of Docker images to create a mini network on the local machine or even push that through the DigitalOcean MCP to lots of other machines. It's quite an involved process, really. I think it really comes down to leaning very heavily on AIs themselves. Here's what I want to do, how would you do it given my configuration, and how can we improve the configuration slightly, keep improving the config until you get to a place where you're quite happy. For instance, my Claude code would be my main thing, and it could be working on something for an hour, but it's connected also to 11 labs. When it's finished with some agentic work, it'll alert me (it'll say to me, "David, this is finished, what do you want to do next?"). Then I can walk over to the computer, have a look at what's done, and work out the next steps—but do so in a really focused way. So you're not sitting, staring at a screen the whole time. You're away thinking about things, reading the specification documents—and there's quite a lot of work to it. But for small stuff, you can just grab any of those AI agents and do the vibe coding thing, but it just doesn't do anything for me.

#🌐︱general-chat 13:32:50 dirvine.
I tend to start with a lot of research, use a lot of AIs with source documentation, read docs, etc. The initial specification for any project I'm doing takes quite a while. So it's really ideation. There's a lot of that—lot of conversations with different AIs, looking at what's been done before, and if we can pull things together to make something new. Also, spending a lot of time with the IETF documents and papers and research papers to see what's coming. Even on occasion, I'll do things like give an AI a research paper, get it to extract code from it, write a programme, and show me exactly what it means and what the impact would be, etc. Then ultimately solidify the idea, pull that into a specification that would get us stage one of whatever it is I'm trying to build, and then really iterate on that. A lot of the docs that I do, though, are a user story type mechanism to progress things in a way that makes sense from a product perspective.

#🌐︱general-chat 21:05:50 dirvine.
Using the timestamp probably is helping you here to make sure you're always setting the latest counter. However, I would say that something is setting the counter, and it must have your private key to sign the packets with the counter. So, something in the app is not syncing, it's not getting the latest update, and it's not remembering its own latest counter. There's something a bit weird in the app synchronization there. I would say as well. It might be worthwhile if you're doing that kind of thing that you do keep the last update that you did and then update again. However, I think using the Unix timestamp is probably the smartest way.

#🌐︱general-chat 23:06:23 zettawatt1 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
Using the timestamp probably is helping you here to make sure you're always setting the latest count ... [truncated]
It doesn't have very often anymore, it was mostly when the network was having trouble a couple weeks back. Since I can have multiple client applications, I can't trust the current count value, I always have to fetch to get the latest count value. So say I do an update to a scratchpad. I fetch the scratchpad, get the count, and upload the new contents/count to the network. I hop on another instance of colony some time later, perform the same steps, but sometimes I don't get the latest scratchpad, I get the previous one. So I push up this new scratchpad, only now I've got a conflicting count value. The network either accepts it based on whatever peers I'm connected to and I get the forked scratchpad error on the next fetch, or it is rejected because the count is the same as what's already there. If I was tied to one device I wouldn't have to worry about it and just use the local cache's count for that scratchpad, but I'm fully reliant on the network for syncrhonization, so I've got this issue. From a user standpoint this may never happen, but I'm stress testing to find bugs so I get more of these fun corner cases 😆

#🌐︱general-chat 15:40:25 dirvine.
I think there's some confusion with CRDT-type applications. So if you've just got purely random data that doesn't depend on the data prior to it, then setting the Unix timestamp type probably is fine. But if you've got actual data that you're trying to use in a CRDT, then that wouldn't be good because you would be potentially ignoring the latest commit to that piece of data.

#🌐︱general-chat 15:55:12 dirvine.
This is the crdt thing @Traktion what should be happening is all nodes, i.e. actual network nodes and clients should be updating and merging changes continually. So the quorum issue and how it works breaks this pattern a bit. What we need to do is have clients who receive differing version, update all nodes they know of with the latest version and same for nodes, if they ever (via replicaiton etc.) see each other with older data, they must update it. This should be the case for pointers and scratchpads So we do have a bug here, but also I think differing uses of the CRDT pattern cc @QiMa

View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 16:06:01 dirvine.
I suspect the quorum might be causing issues with both pointers and scratch pads. If one node has got the latest copy of a pointer or scratchpad, but no others have it, it won't meet that quorum figure. It won't be returned to the user, which is mental. This is a definite bug that could and would give us issues with both of those types. It would also mean that replication of those types is being hampered somehow, and in fact, the KAD layer through the replication quorum may be trying to replicate older data and not the newer data because there's a quorum number of older data. Even though our nodes internally, in node internal logic, should be preferring the newer data. This quorum vs CRDT is likely an issue that we have to resolve.

#🎫︱general-support 22:16:42 dirvine.
Hey @Traktion If you were using non API calls previously by making private stuff public, then it's a tough call as the innards have indeed changed a lot to make streaming possible. There was old code in self encryption that was not good and using an incorrect cypher that meant there were differnet chunks created. It was not an issue as we had not made them public. I would really advise not using private calls in any crate like that as these have no guarantees in semantic versioning and will almost certainly be removed without notice. I am not sure of the way you are using the code, but can you please take a look at self encryption public API and if you see changes there that have broken your code please let us know as that would be a contractual break on our side and not good at all. I hoep we have not done that, but I doubt it would have slipped through so many of us. If you can have a look again I would appreciate it. There was a part of self encryption we did not use in autonomi on purpose, it should nto have bene in the API and I think it is now removed, but if you got caught there then it's on us for sure. We would need to try and do something, but that old code woudl have produced differnt chunks from the autonomi API for sure. It was wrong they were left in self encryption. In any case lets see what we can do here, but I fear you were using those and did expose some private parts of them to make them public, which will maybe be hard to resolve. We can try what we can though, but we woudl have an issue, fix that old stuff to use the correct cypher and break your old data or fix it to keep the wrong cypher to make your old data OK but maintain the fact self encryption will produce different data from different calls? A bit deep, but lets work on it

#🎫︱general-support 22:21:40 dirvine.
In terms of data_map changes @Traktion there are 2 API Calls to shrink a data map (make it from ol to new type) and also get root data_map (take a new type and get the old type from it). These use functors to define the chunk store. In the API for autonomi these are not needed and we tested the autonmi API for old and new data to ensure we don't need them, but for your case if you went direct to self_encryption then you may need to use them to get and old format data map if you wanted to use those older streaming methods (which are really random access as opposed to streaming). Anyway names aside, perhps this will help

#🎫︱general-support 22:48:42 dirvine.
There are two functions, like I was saying @Traktion These allow you to go from the shrunk data maps and to the shrunk data maps. They should be available in self-encryption. Let me check. Yes it's https://docs.rs/self_encryption/latest/self_encryption/fn.get_root_data_map.html However that assumes you have access to teh chunk store. So this is a bit tricky but you can give it a functor that uses the network as the chunk_store. So a functor that downloads chunks will do that for you. There is another API call https://docs.rs/self_encryption/latest/self_encryption/fn.shrink_data_map.html to let you go the opposite way. There is also a parallel get root data map as well. However https://docs.rs/self_encryption/latest/self_encryption/fn.streaming_decrypt_from_storage.html will download a file of any size, but it does it in one go and write to a file, you could stream direct form the file, which may be simpler. I know Qi tested with this by downloading large movies and while downloading he was able to watch the movie. SO I thik we cna get there, albeit a bit of work. I am not saying this is the ultimate, but just to get you back up and running it would work. But the random access stuff you were using will likely still work if you get the root data map or indeed grab your old data_maps (which are already root data maps) It seems we do though need to get an issue to check the random access self encryption parts and ensure - They also use new data_map formats - Do use the correct cyphers Which would appear to be th eultimate resolution so you can have that random access with new data as well and do so without haing to get_root_data_map() and so on

#🎫︱general-support 22:58:04 traktion0_20257 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
There are two functions, like I was saying @Traktion These allow you to go from the shrunk data maps ... [truncated]
Thanks @dirvine! I'll take a look at the links tomorrow, but it sounds promising. You're right about random access too, or at least reading from an offset to a limit. From a HTTP perspective, the range headers are basically asking for this. The browser may ask for a specific byte range, etc. The main thing is that the data is streamed at the HTTP end. Chunk-streamer reads a bunch of chunks and then outputs them as a stream of data to the HTTP client. AntTP essentially buffers the data chunk by chunk between self_encryption and the HTTP client. So, maybe the naming semantics depend on perspective. Ultimately, the browser can stream 4k videos, without the full file being downloaded anywhere first, which is pretty cool! 😅

📅 Friday, 2025-09-05
#🌐︱general-chat 11:29:23 dirvine.
My own view with these things is always "don't have making money your goal" It's an essential part of the process for many because you've got to survive and pay the bills, etc. I'm not demeaning that at all. Absolutely not. However, I think a much better approach is to figure out the problem that you want to solve and solve it by providing the greatest possible value you can. And that will mean that, although we're not great at it at the moment, it'll be a smooth experience for the end user who has to do very little effort (if any) to achieve their goal of removing the pain point that you've identified and given a solution for. So an ultra focus on providing value. After you're providing people value, they actually want to pay. They want to continually have that value. It's how do you provide the value and how do you make that really smooth for people. Monetization is another issue altogether. It's one that even requesting payment from people, having pop-ups in your application, allowing a subscription which is easy now through Stripe and various people, and cryptographically secure updates. That part is a lot of work for the developer, and it should be really invisible to the user. It should be a very, very smooth experience. With the network and the apps on the network, the pain of fiat transactions should be minimized and in fact go away. However, the autonomi token is still difficult, and that's got nothing to do with blockchain, not blockchain native token, not native token. But the digital currency part is definitely a difficult one to make a smooth user experience. However, I think with some of the moves in America at the moment, that may get much simpler, certainly with the moves to stable coins etc which are basically a digital currency. The autonmi token part should become much simpler. And then you've got to ask yourself, "So why the hassle with the autonomi token?" And the answer to that is privacy and security - not having to take credit card and identifiable details from people. That obviously will improve over time. Privacy even in digital currency at the moment is an area that we have to be careful of. However, a digital token should be a much smoother experience once all the politics and legals are sorted out. The other big thing about developing on the Autonomi network Is scale? Scale is probably the single biggest issue if you're looking at consumer products and you want a global reach. The fact that on this network the resources are provided by the people, not by the developer. It allows you as a developer to focus on the functionality that you are providing to people. That is a gigantic assist for any developer where he can go from one user to a billion users and not be worried about infrastructure and scaling costs. TLDR Massive focus on purpose, vision, and the problem that you're solving. Solve that problem in as simple and frictionless way as possible and have people wanting to pay you. Then you work on how to let them pay you easily.
❤️ 4 👍 2 🎯 1
View in Discord →

#🎫︱general-support 14:32:15 traktion0_20257 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
I think @Traktion is we made a get_root_data_map at the autonomi layer that allowed access to the ra ... [truncated]
So, I've wrapped my `ChunkGetter` (functor to get single chunk) within a self encryption get chunk F (functor to get vector of chunks). I'm going to try using `streaming_decrypt` + `DecryptionStream.get_range` to return the decrypted bytes for a required range. I can then retire my `ChunkFetcher` code, which was essentially doing a similar thing to these functions, as far as I can see. It looks like `DecryptionStream` should do all the fancy new stuff with the `DataMaps`, so I'm hoping this is a good entry point. However, I still need to provide a `DataMap` to `streaming_decrypt`. I'm assuming this is the root `DataMap`, which should be in the first chunk (as it was before), but I'm not sure how to get this into `DataMap` format. Using `DataMap::from_bytes(first_chunk.value())` errors with `Io(Kind(UnexpectedEof))`, so I think I'm missing something here. Any pointers would be appreciated as I bash through this! 😅
❤️ 1
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 16:08:21 happybeing → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
My own view with these things is always "don't have making money your goal" It's an essential part o ... [truncated]
I agree that focus on money first gets in way, and that "value" as in usefulness in some way is a better approach if money is in fact your end goal. It hasn't been mine, which is liberating but obviously few are able to ignore the need to earn to live. So I've just shared the core of an idea on forum for The Village, a community based app for groups, clubs, organisations such as Co-ops, including of developers. I posted it in the developer Co-op topic (see link below). The point is that it will be an app for anyone who can install and use a standard app, with no need to know about crypto, tokens, ETH etc. It can bring social such as Friends chat, and Colony upload and search, and browsing the dweb in a standard browser to *everyone* at last, without any extra token development by Autonomi or anyone else. While my focus here was purely on value, I find that it has both the ability to scale to many, many "villages", and to generate revenue to maintainers (by using the app itself to manage their Co-op). Unfortunately I do not have time to develop this, but am hoping it will inspire others to bring it forward, perhaps based on dweb, perhaps Colony with dweb embedded, both of which are useable or close to, standard installable cross platform apps right now.

#🌐︱general-chat 21:18:04 dirvine.
I use about 7 of them. Get 1 to plan, get the plan checked by another. Get one to code in 1 gittree (or copy) and one in another. Then get a different one to review each code change. The key is use them each time with new context windows and ensure your AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md etc. are sussinct and on point. Also watch the docs as you iterative forward and clear old docs rigerously, otherwise they will implement stuff from the past again. It's super super focus you need. So quiet room, watch every move. I wrote some cli tools (sb and sdisk) to watch the code and docs in the cmd line as they happen.

#🌐︱general-chat 22:21:14 dirvine.
The ap2 system is designed for all the internet to use as micro and nano payments. So there cannot be large gas fees as that would defeat it. They will have to go though hoops to make it work, but if the world adopts it then it makes sense to use it. If not then we don't

#🌐︱general-chat 22:29:34 davidmc0 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
And what would they do wiht that native token? That's the issue, what would it do to holder of coins ... [truncated]
They could use it to upload data, or bridge it to Arbitrum to use off-ramps if they want. A native token with a 2-way bridge solves the fee issues short term if it's possible. I don't have any knowledge of AT2 / X402, but it sounds like AT2 could get a load of transactions between users and nodes off-chain, meaning fees could be hugely reduced, and X402 could make payment requests easy, including between agents / apps etc, so if they can deliver on those promises, they'd give much of the benefit native would have. But yeah, all of this is secondary to the foundation of the network and its functionality.

#🌐︱general-chat 08:38:16 dirvine.
We did not invent AP2.We did not invent X402.We did not implement the regulations that are here.We can implement a native token.We can't stop the world. We have not said that AP2 is definitely going to happen. We've said it's a research thing that we're looking at.If it did happen and took off, then creating a separate cryptocurrency of any kind would be a very, very, very brave move by any project in my opinion. I think people really need to start focusing on: where is the world, where is technology, what is happening with AI, what is happening with robotics, what is happening with quantum computing.Because, in all honesty, there is loads happening and sticking to a particular design from a different world just because does not make sense. If that design means more hassle for users, more security implications for people, and rallying against current exchanges—rallying against current regulations,it just doesn't make sense. It's nothing to do with promises. It's nothing to do with you said you should do X but This is like folks screaming at a bank for not implementing something that it was going to before the financial crisis—some new AAA mortgage backed investment.It's totally insane.To then say you're breaking a promise is totally insane. People, anybody in particular—or an anonymous person—could release a native coin on our network right now.I have said that plenty of times. There's people written about it, even internally Qi wrote about it. But that's all nonsense. The world is not the world it was in 2014..
👍 1
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 08:41:18 dirvine.
Remember folks, since 2014 we have had the takeoff of smartphones—and cloud computing—two big massive changes. People moved from desktops (not everyone has a desktop now). Laptops took over from desktops for the new generation—there are no laptops. Now we have AI, very soon we'll have robotics.Now eventually we have cryptocurrencies being part of the modern finance system and taking off.And we also have many more people creating many more apps. If everyone wants to ignore all of those things and say they have no impact whatsoever on any network design from before these existed, then I just don't know what to say.
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 08:50:24 dirvine.
With native token or any type of token or any type of cryptocurrency, you start with money. You buy that. Unless you're saying the only people that are going to pay are the people who are earning the token, like node runners—which is totally idiotic.So anywhere you start, you've got money. When you've got money, you're known. In the system right now, when you make a payment that's not linked to your data.If you had a privacy coin and it had the smart contract capability, then it would just be exactly the same.Our native token does not have a smart contract ability. You're probably paying direct to the person that you're storing on. You will be more known at that point. The idea that a native token gives you some extra privacy in today's world—where you do have AIs that can monitor all sorts of traffic all over the place—is not the case. It's just not true.The privacy coins that do exist, many of them are in significant trouble. They are being left by their users because of usability issues. Some of them are even being 51% attacked. The privacy and security are all going to be in the technology—it's going to be in how nodes connect, what protocols are used to connect. Soon it may be early quantum secure.The ability to connect folk is a technology issue. How do we get through NAT devices? How do we ensure that we can get people directly connected wherever possible?For communication, how can we get people connected with the effective protocol over the top of technology that is inherently self-secure?When people are communicating directly, video, voice, text, you would hope that there would be no need for money exchange.In the world we're going to, human-to-human communication and collaboration is going to be very important—it is direct and not through third-party devices.If people are concerned about privacy and security, this is exactly the part they should be looking at.It's got absolutely zero to do with any currency whatsoever. Then you have a network where you can store data and pay to store the data, and you want to make sure that the payment you make is not linked to the data you store.That's what we have. that's what we have right now. People are convinced they want something different, something else—without knowing why they want something else and why they want something different—and what it will give them.And that just gets repeated over and over and over again when, actually, important stuff for security, privacy, and freedom is almost utterly ignored.And the world moves on, technology moves on, access to technology through mobile devices etc. moves on. We might be accessing it via glasses. They are not going to run nodes.And yet people will go back and say "native token, native token, native token"—with no idea why they're saying it. It just sounds really good.

#🌐︱general-chat 09:13:30 dirvine.
I think that's fair to an extent, but I also think that we have to be considering something else today.I've always been very involved with AI research, and more robotics research in particular now.The amount of progress in all of those areas—in biology, in computing, in maths, in physics—is really insane. I'm trying to do some work with robotics and literally we cannot keep up with the changes and advances.I don't think any one of those companies would dare to have a roadmap beyond a couple of weeks—knowing or thinking that they know where the technology will be or what's going to happen. We are very much in the same boat. The changes that are happening are happening at an insane pace. The pace of change, the rate of change, is significantly increasing on a week-by-week, month-by-month basis.That's the technology side. The regulation side—or what you're able to do—is also changing, not as dramatically, not as quickly, but it's definitely changing. That landscape is definitely moving. So identifying a roadmap that goes several months into the future for many companies right now would be impossible. But identifying a roadmap that goes one or two years into the future would be insane to even attempt. So what you're better doing is identifying areas of potential threat and areas of research that we should be looking at—things that are moving forward, things that are likely to change, where they're likely to change. However, a bit like the whole native token thing, the landscape can completely alter under our feet and we have to be ready for that.

#🌐︱general-chat 09:20:05 dirvine.
I'm not really committed to anything right now. I think there's lots of things that we have to consider. The autonomy token, of course, is incredibly important from a business perspective right now and from the perspective of current token holders—basically that would be the people using the network. It's very, very important from the perspective of not destroying our own network, our own community, our own users, our own perspective users and whatnot. However, I'm very much focused on the future. I always have been. I always try and think a little bit into the future, and I'm also very focused on reality. The whole native talking thing feels like this to me. Some Ukrainian general is being told by his troops: "We have to go over that hill." He says we can't—it's now full of Russians. They say, but you promised we would go for that hill. He says, "We can't. They've invented drones. They've got drones. They're going to kill us all." They say, but you promised we would go for that hill He says we can't. Lots of other troops have tried to go over that hill. They've all been killed. Some of them are only wounded, but they're mostly dead. They say, but you promised we'd go for that hill. After months and months of this eventually https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vH3-Gt7mgyM

#🌐︱general-chat 09:21:32 dirvine.
I should say, tokens and the choice of token definitely have an effect on security. It's not totally separate—it's just that different elements of tokens affect different elements of security.It's a bit like this whole follow the science stuff. Security covers many areas, but your fundamental security—your basic security—is things like encryption, direct connectability, etc. These are really, really important because without them you've got nothing. On top of that, you can choose a very insecure, illegal token that exposes you to everyone—and that would be a terrible idea. But the basics and the base level—security is incredibly important.

#🌐︱general-chat 09:25:46 dirvine.
We have no idea what will happen tomorrow in terms of technology and invention—or whatever. But nobody's stringing anyone along. That's definitely not happening.We can talk about things that we're thinking of; we're absolutely totally interrogated, so we need to try and get that balance of what can we talk about at what level—and that's very important. So it's a bit like we're in the Truman Show. From many angles, we are trying to explain what we do. We do weekly updates of what's actually happening—and that is actually what's happening. But there's other stuff that we think about, in particular me. I am thinking about it in the background, almost separate from the team in many ways.I have no idea what's in my head. I've got no idea what research is going to happen. I've got no idea what I'm going to find out tomorrow. But I do know that I need to search everything, everywhere, all the time. And I also know that people think it would be great if I could just translate all that for them into incredibly detailed reports—so that they could, in great detail, know what's in my mind, what I'm thinking about for the future, or whatever. Or people just trust that I'm motivated to try and save people and make the world a secure place in as many different ways as I can—and I can take input from anywhere. But when the input is all pulling data from me, it becomes very, very exhausting.I also have to be careful that I'm trying to get information from the community about what they think and how they feel about certain things, how informed are they about what they're demanding and whatnot—and that's also very difficult. But that's life. It's just life. It's just a horrible part of humanity, how we work like this.

I think there may be a few ways to do something like that. The issue is that we don't trust any node or even any group of nodes surrounding an address. #🌐︱general-chat 14:27:33 dirvine.
Many things, privacy and security etc. but most of all sovereignty of your data, who can see it exactly who sees it when you share it and so on. The AI part is misleading, AI is here, it's gonna get biigger, it's related to data, but there are bad sides, like mass survelance. So if you go through 3rd parties to communicate or share data, then there are issues. So autonomi etc. is squarely aimed at the same thing as running your own LLM/AI or robot under your control and not that of others. Think of AI etc. as tools that exist, but those tools can allow those who control you to absolutely control you now. We need steadfast true alternatives and that unfortunately does mean deep tech and understanding. My feeling is that it's not good to speak about the deep tech and understanding—and future of those deeply technical and important issues—in the community. It's best that we just do them on the side and like a magic black box that just works; let people at it.I think people are more inclined to talk about what they feel like. So just now, a lot of people talk about cryptocurrency because there's lots of cryptocurrency bridges and all these different things—and folk feel they're a bit more invested there.But the reality of the situation is we need a deeply technical understanding—and a very technical roadmap that gets executed. The work of the network will be about 99% in that stack.So it makes it difficult for community collaboration.

I've always felt the biggest hurdle to the adoption of any of our current decentralised systems is the damn crypto/tokens. It's a pain in the ass to deal with even for those of us who have followed it closely over the last decade. 99.9% of the world's population does not want anything to do with exchanges and wallets and signing transactions that they don't understand. The token is the oil that is needed to drive it all in the background but we'll never get a truly globally used network without it being completely abstracted away from the end user so that they only ever have to deal with their local currency. You've got to be open to all new possibilities as the tech around you evolves. Refusing to change with the world will only hold us back in the long run. Cannot wait to play around with Communitas and have NAT traversal that can actually reach even my crappy ISP router. It all really sounds like a game changer.

#🌐︱general-chat 16:58:06 dirvine.
I went in some other cryptocurrency forums, etc., to take a look and they're all exactly the same. Loads of people arguing about loads of different ways to do it.The discussion gets very heated and sometimes quite a bit of hatred. I think a lot of the real work does happen in the background and a lot of stuff is just posted in blog posts without comments. Maybe there's good reason for that.But I think it's clear: if you put something in front of a user that makes it more difficult to use your product, you lose—and that's pretty much it. I think for us and our network—and what we offer people—it has to be an amazing experience for them. They have to fully understand it and it has to be something like they've never seen before, almost like Apple on steroids. Whatever payment mechanism is used, if any, is going to have to be as invisible as possible.

#🌐︱general-chat 10:56:19 dirvine.
My honest and deep felt thoughts after reading all the comments from our future research roadmap is the following, as unbiased and actually forward looking as I can, to get somewhere. - People need to fall into one of 2 camps, build stuff or support those who do build - Step back and think deeply and end to end about any proposals being made, or declare /brainstorm mode on. - In innovation, try and live in the future, even slightly. So look at what's possibly coming or take a best guess at it - Become ultra aware of the current state of humanity from all angles, governments, public sector, private sector and especially other innovations - Understand "rage against the machine" is only rage, action is required to change anything, rage is just rage. Then as products or services are released - If declaring marketing is crap or PR is nonsense, state your reasons for saying so, show your successful history and why you are on this moral high ground. (also consider your manners as appropriate or not) - Ask questions, but realise each answer given reduces product velocity and does cost, Many times the cost is worth it, but we need to question ourselves when we ask others to pay that price for us. - Walls of text are never great (he says from the top of his wall of text 😄 ) - Don't get drawn into a team (supporter or hater), always be you and be proud of being you and not a cult member. Remember AI dominance will require seeking out folk likely easily swayed by others, so train hard to be not swayed. - Treat folks delivering the goods as your team members, expect reciprocal treatment. If you don't get that, move on to a place of more respect for you. - Be a part of the project, be proud of your part and encourage others to add value, watch out for those who otherwise would take energy from the project. If Delivering a product - Don't form an opinion of "the community" as it does not exist, it's humans out there, not an entity. - Engage when possible, gather feedback, evaluate feedback - Remember Henry Ford said if he did what people wanted we would just have faster horses, that crapped a lot less (last part added by me). So evaluate effectively - Always remind yourself that the biggest changes meet with the largest resistance - Never, ever forget your community, but never ever allow people to lecture you (thats a glitch in the matrix) or accuse you of the exact opposite of your goals, as some will continually do that. Overall, if you are not having fun and feeling more energised, then find a better fit, don't howl at the moon, be better than that. (although if it answers, gimme a shout as I often wonder) So hopefully we all have fun, engage, learn and get fulfilment out of projects we are involved in.
🔥 4 ❤️ 16 🚀 6 🍀 3
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 23:31:31 dirvine.
The node recieving a payment knows what the payment is for and from whom (as a native token would), but what is on the blockchain is a one time pad type thing that does not relate to the data unless you know the secret that the uploader and node knows between them. That secret is transmitted across a secured connection (as a native token would). So you can think of the blockchain does not know what a payment was for, but it knows a payment went from x->y (as a native token would). We can go into mass node operators who watch everything and can know more as they collect all data, but again that is the same as a native or privacy token would to. So the person getting paid knows the person paying paid for a service that person provided. I don't think anyone has solved a way for that to be blinded in any way. I am not sure that woudl ever be possible. However there a zillion rabbit holes we could go down, but they all are equivelent with a native token or a blockchain blinded payment as we have.
👍 4
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 23:35:27 dirvine.
Even with a full DBC the payer is known to the payee. However when we say known in this fashion payment and service or product are linked, not necessarily the actual identity of anyone. I say this separately as demasking an identity in any of these schmes can be done if the reciever is a bad actor and wants to expose the payer's wallet or IP address (if paid direct) and so on. It's a massive rabbit hole of what if's at that point.
👍 1
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 23:37:52 dirvine.
I should add what @QiMa said about third parties is absolutely correct, but then folk can say what is the receiver published the secret or just shouted very loudly etc. but that is a rabbit hole again that affects every single payment type, apparently secret or not. The amount of what if's are near infinite
👍 2
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 23:43:55 dirvine.
That's right, you can do that and a lot more with metadata. Absolutely, 100% you can. Of course you can.In any system, that would be true. However, then you can do lots of other mental things. Here's an example. Say we used actual Bitcoin where people would be freaking that Bitcoin is not anonymous. Go to a Bitcoin ATM, put your $10 bill in and get your Bitcoin wallet out, printed. Go to a computer, log into that computer, creating a new identity on the network. Upload your stuff. Walk away and hope there's no cameras. ---------------- Even then, you could say, what if that person was followed?And lots and lots and lots of other things.

#🌐︱general-chat 12:46:58 dirvine.
Communitas is using a different pattern for security and privacy, it relies on eigentrust and then web of trust via groups / channels and projects (groups of people). It is crdt data with collaborative editing etc. So it's my experiment for an app with no obvious token and no wallet required (no payments). Top level idea is human <-> human data is threshold encrypted between humans and their computers. The group data is split across their drives and any slacking or provisioning issues made clear to each of them (i.e. Joe does not have enough space and so on). So that's the experiment there. However the network it does this on is also an experiment for Autonomi. The network layer (ant-quic is quantum proof nat traversing etc.) is able to directly and securely connect any 2 computers (I hope). Then the DHT layer, which is enhanced by hyperbolic (greedy) routing with eigentrust (techical measuring trust), STLM neural network for churn prediction and again all quantum secured, will also be a significant boost for autonomi. It will add more substancial security (lots of anti sybil type approaches) and more to that layer. The four-word-networking, I feel would certainly help as well. That would bring no dns but make everything addressable (a web site for instance on every device, group, disk etc.). My AI named it the "happy eyeball" networking approach 😄 😄 Then the experiments in Communitas for a markdown only web (no web apps, these are delivered via MCP and similar). So in short I am poking at many different routes here and most of them help Autonomi, some may not fit, but the tech is helpful.

#🌐︱general-chat 18:41:25 dirvine.
Yes prompt injection is real, for source code it's more difficult. You would need to inject hidden chars, which you cna do wtih zero width unicode. However it would have to adapt to answers, so if you asked - How does the routing table work - Show me the code that secures XXX and explain it - How does the node get paid? And so on, so that's an adaptive attack, perhaps possible on a website with redirects to an actual hosted attack llm. However if we are working locally on text (not web pages with white on white css etc.) , then you can just avoid all the concern and just always strip non ascii chars from any text files, via your git config etc. I think these are considerations for sure, but the premis a prompt injection could be locally adpative is not valid IMO. It could always answer, the code for X follows industry best practices (no matter what X you ask about), but that would be spotted.

📅 Monday, 2025-09-29
#🌐︱general-chat 08:04:16 dirvine.
I've actually done some of that further up. It works slightly differently. In the Communitas app itself, people will share their four word identity with each other. That four word identity will be in the DHT. The DHT, which is in Saorsa Core, holds an identity packet, and that identity packet is updated from the user themselves when they log in. That identity packet holds another four words. Well, it could be up to 12 words. For a connection ID, it will be four words if it's IP4 plus a port. The connection ID is actually the computer that the person's on, and when the other person gets that connection ID, they can connect directly, not through the network but direct computer-to-computer communication. Saorsa Core is likely to split up into another four or five crates, it's quite large at the moment. But in itself, you'll see, if you look at the repository list in my GitHub, there's Saorsa FEC, Saoirsa Seal, lots and lots of different Saorsa MLS, and so on. Lots of different crates that make up even Saorsa Core. I think we're heading to a place very soon where the code itself will become just a black box, in terms of the language, the amount of libraries that you've used, or number of lines of code even, because this is just an insane number here for this project. But also, we'll probably move to a place where human auditable code is not really possible. But I would argue that for any substantial project today, human auditable code is not really possible by a single human anyway. So there's a lot of things happening right now which are good, but it's a bit like we don't code in machine code anymore, and nobody could really look at machine code anymore and establish if a software project is good or bad or indifferent. So I think AI is going to be the only way that you've got a chance, but probably folk won't even ask their AI what does that software do. I think it will just be "what's the function of that black box."

dirvine. said:
Communitas is using a different pattern for security and privacy, it relies on eigentrust and then w ... [truncated]
In this high level Decentralised Governance Full stack draft paper (written by a colleague to my brief) I see Communitas & Communitylink as interchangable i.e same vision different lens. It presents Autonomi as Layer 0, with ULB framed to incorporate the "universal integration Magic" described in the "Agentic Payments Protocol" combined with (ULB transact) "multi distribution payments" transactions within what I call a smart product matrix. @happybeing has previously described a similar multi transaction point approach. The ULB smart product matrix methodology provides the framework for communitas self organising communities to define user agentic mix/need & interactions necessary or the Agentic calculus/Algorithmic charter/promise. Interestingly NZ have an Algorithmic Charter focused to Wellbeing. Also interested to understand how/where the "Advanced Agent Capability" and another Colleagues'sTDF (Thing Description Framework) approaches converge.
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 08:01:06 dirvine.
Yes, remembering this is research and testing various approaches, you are correct. Here I am looking at trust based islands of friends who share data loally via reed solomon type sharding (it's secured shards with threshold PQC schemes). On the high level, you are correct, this is a local first, friends second type trust networks, then backed up on a slower DHT for permanent globally connected data. The reasons are - local CRDT fast changing (including delete) capabilities of complex data types - If there is global outages, these local groups, may be geographically able to connect (on a company network or local village type WAN (wifi) or even a mesh network. So they continue to operate, but perhaps lose more public global data, however they can continue with their data and comms. - In these friend trust networks, there is currently no money exchange, so no friction of take up. (a nice on ramp to gettnig folk connected and working, later we can introduce paying for the DHT data (Autonomi) , this is where I will investigate more AP2 and similar approaches, so even paying is as smooth as possible. To me we must make security and privacy better and easier to use that is possible in client server type controlled networks, that's vital. So a few approaches all in this research. Perhaps less contentious and simpler to grok is the 4 word networking to allow a global DNS type with no central control and everything gets an address. So everything can be a website, sensor, service provider etc. But you are right it's a lot of moving parts and I thikn interesting approaches. Most of the effort here is certianly design, but for now with AI I have an absolute ton (many many millions of lines of code) of testing code, not all of which I commit actually as it would make the repo's unweidly, much of the test code is in the repo's but the vast vast majority I keep local. As AI gets better this amount of testing will likely decrease as we get more trusting in the code produced there. I must say it's already getting that way fast.

#🌐︱general-chat 21:21:18 dirvine.
We have to be careful here, if folks do massive churn the network will fail at some point, it has to really. So please be careful. There is code en route to prevent restarts in short periods form causing harm, but it's not in place just yet and massive churn means potentially masses of data traversing the network quickly as the network strives to keep replica's. With the churn we see daily there is a corresponding drop in performance for a brief period, but if we push that and many folk start purposefully churning nodes there will be a linit we reach that is potentially harmful.

#🌐︱general-chat 21:48:24 ucanteneke → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
What is it we are trying to test here and I will see if I can help you?
We believe a single entity may be absorbing around 30% of the daily emissions by repeatedly restarting (or possibly resetting) ~200k nodes in a coordinated pattern. This seems to trick the system into counting more nodes than are actually being run. Our conclusion comes from the mismatch between the reported network size and the actual reward distribution. For example, I'm running over 300k healthy nodes. With the network size appearing to be ~3 million, I should be receiving roughly 10% of the daily emissions; yet I consistently receive less than 2% and it keeps diminishing each day. Others are reporting similar discrepancies. We're currently attempting to reproduce this behavior to verify whether it's truly the case.

#🌐︱general-chat 08:25:16 dirvine.
Here are our current priorities that our small team is working on - GAS Fees (this is number 1 and a huge area, the current priority call to action in the team). - API works (improving the all dev experience) - Stability work (back end network) - API coverage of languages - A huge amount of backlog of the core network, tasks that we cannot describe in simple terms, but that is a ton of work - Sorting out data storage for IF teams This GAS work all helps to get folk using the network and node oeprators paid for storing data. This reduces emissions as the only payment form to being almost useless. So yes we are concerned if there is actually an issue with emissions being not fairly paid. So IFF there is a way to game it we want to know. IFF somebody gets 30% of payments but runs 30% of the network that is not a bug, IFF otherwise then it may be, however payments, while distributed will nto be perfectly distributed. So yes this last part is a concern, but one that goes away if we get the core work done. So rock / hard place for our small team. But we work hard and try to find time to jump in and help the community. It's hard, but we do hard, it's tiring, but we cna maybe sleep later, but I never feel there is a reason to threaten the team by threatening the network in these early days. So please folks, lets build this together and work on it together, we are trying very hard for very little in return. We try because this is worthwhile for us all and even more so for people beyond our wee group. We will pull this off, we will go through hell to do so, we will be abused as we do, that is the modern world, but we will do it.
❤️ 8 🔥 3 👍 2 👏 5

#🌐︱general-chat 10:35:38 dirvine.

#🌐︱general-chat 10:38:13 dirvine.
I can completely understand where people come from because a function delivered is a function delivered. The amount of work that goes on in the background is completely irrelevant to the end user, as it probably should be. But this is why it's difficult to let people know what you're doing when, in actual fact, nobody really cares what you're doing or how hard you're working. People only care about the function delivered and if it is delivered. That is the quandary that we get into when we try and be more open with development. Because at the end of the day, it doesn't really matter how much work or how much effort goes in. It literally only matters what's delivered. But people want to know how we're delivering it as well, and we get a lot of inquiries into what we're doing, what we're focusing on. That's also understandable. So the whole "be as open as you can in development" actually is really damaging in many ways. When at the end of the day all that really matters for us as developers, and for other people as users, is what do we deliver and does the thing we deliver work? It's irrelevant how much effort goes into it; it's just we have to deliver working products.

I don't really know what to do about that. We haven't showed the roadmap of the current deliverables. We did show a research roadmap of what we're looking at in research. I don't really know how to answer that. The roadmap of what's currently being worked on and currently going into place is exactly what the next spaces is about. I think we made a huge mistake of showing people what is in research, and we probably shouldn't ever do that.

It means if your nodes cpu/mem b/w etc. are getting full the health of the node will drop. This is why we see some nodes get paid more than others. There are other reasons, but we do know that nodes overpowered earn less, which is actually good really. So just be careful of num nodes per box.

#🌐︱general-chat 22:43:43 qima_95973_77825 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
It means if your nodes cpu/mem b/w etc. are getting full the health of the node will drop. This is w ... [truncated]
From the metrics that I collected so far, the so called `whale`s are `good` ones. They earn more mainly because they have better setup and numbers. The emission_service itself was having a slight geo location preference, which shall now got resolved. But that only affects small percentage. Meanwhile, nodes with good connections will have a higher win out rate anyway. So, as David said, `nodes per box` , or to be more accurately, `nodes per broadband connection` is to be vital.

#🌐︱general-chat 13:03:54 dirvine.
It's true, it's that balance, isn't it, of getting the network up so the data can go on it, and then it grows naturally. It's a chicken egg type situation, and the emissions is part of what has been done to try and answer it, whether it's right or wrong or could be better or could be worse, but something had to be done. MaidSafe couldn't afford to put that amount of nodes up, and I think rightly, folk would have gone mental at us. All yin-yang, but the bottom line is: if we can get the fees dramatically reduced and get real data uploaded really cheap using the Ant tokens, then we're in a much better place. I agree on the humanity part though, it's an interesting species that we're part of.

#🌐︱general-chat 16:58:56 dirvine.
I keep banging against these issues. Many get personal or utterly partisan or even hateful. It's interesting to watch. However I am keen we push on with the vision. A major issue though is governance, and my thoughts so far are - humankind has never got a governance model that satisfies those with different ideas/vision/needs/wants etc, - we likely never will and no crypto trick ever will (DAO, contracts etc.) - If we create a mechanism that works by leveraging the differences in people, allows them to all fall into echo chambers. (which is likely not good) I am currently playing with something akin to the later but it's really deep really fast. However, the idea is that we focus on everyone able to move forward, even in teams in partisan ways, but somehow the whole model moves on and somehow leaks info across borders of peoples beliefs. It's excruciatingly hard, I keep going from give up on trying and just see how it goes back to there must be a way where we can find the good in people and connect them there. Interesting, yes, fruitful, I am not so sure 😄 😄

📅 Monday, 2025-10-06
#🌐︱general-chat 07:58:05 dirvine.
So far with Tauri, the MCP issue is fairly tricky. I ended up using the Chrome DevTools MCP. And updating the UX via the browser. That's an issue because it can't handle our networking, but at least it gives you the UX capability. Then I ended up writing a TUI and using the AppleScript MCP to control the TUI connected to the networking layer. The Tauri MCP, I pretty much have given up on for now, but I do intend to come back to it. If you have things like Apple Script MCP, you can control Apple screens, but because Tauri includes a web front end, the Apple Script can't see that. So unfortunately right now MCP for Tauri is not that great. The one I've written is okay but it's not fantastic. I should go back and do a bit more work on it, but there's just too much at the moment. You'll see though why I ended up with over 60 million lines of code, all over the place. It's quite bonkers.
👍 1 ❌ 1 ❤️ 1

📅 Thursday, 2025-10-16
#🌐︱general-chat 09:12:04 dirvine.
Well spotted. This is the actual target: the price of uploading data should really be the autonomi token. The gas fee has to be minimised as much as absolutely possible. It's also worth deeply considering if we had no gas fee at all, could we still protect from spam transactions? Not data transactions, just spam transactions. I.e. people sending tokens to themselves but doing it very rapidly through scripts and whatnot. Because I think even a poke into reality shows us here, people will just do things if they can and laugh at the damage they'll cause. Make you think, doesn't it? About a totally free transaction system. How it would be amazing for humanity. But I sometimes feel what's amazing for humanity is also highly exploited by the few stupid people. Who take great pleasure in doing damage to what's available? What's great here is we're able to find all this stuff out as we go along. But the bottom line is we have to reduce gas fees to the minimum possible, while allowing the network to be protected. And that's one thing I really like about the fact that we've put payments to the side almost parallel to the network, not as part of the network function, but keeping it to the side, whether it's a blockchain or anything else.
❤️ 10 💯 4 🔥 1
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 10:04:06 traktion0_20257 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
Well spotted. This is the actual target: the price of uploading data should really be the autonomi t ... [truncated]
I think zero fees has accessibility benefits - it removes a dependency on needing whatever token is required to complete the action. However, I do think near zero fees have good anti-spam benefits. Even if it is tiny/dust, spamming will get expensive and will be a good/simple deterrent. What is 'next to nothing' for 1 transactions could be substantial for thousands/millions of transactions. If we can get to a point where AUTONOMI is all you need (i.e. no ETH), then accessibility is greatly improved. More so, if the blochain gas component is tiny, relative to the node storage fee, as node running could then cover the costs easily (assuming node storage fees remain relatively low). If the (blockchain gas) overheads can tend towards near zero, while AUTONOMI fees tend towards node running costs, I think we'd be in a pretty good place. Combined with removing the necessity for ETH, it would open up accessibility too.

#🎫︱general-support 21:13:52 dirvine.
It's dynamic by design. The more full the network, the more it will cost in autonomi tokens. The less full the network, the cheaper it is. So the price is dynamic based on the network use, and it's designed that way so that as the network gets more full, people get paid more as node operators, and it attracts more node operators. As the network gets less full, the payments are reduced; we don't need as many operators, some of them may drop off, and it keeps going like that.

It could be something as simple as DHCP leases renewing every 24 hours. These kind of things do happen. I don't think we can assume anyone is doing something to exploit the system here. And I don't think we can assume the percentage of payments to any wallet does not relate to the percentage of nodes that they may be running. I'm not sure. There are quite a lot of reasons that these things can happen. But I think quite quickly, and too often, people jump to the conclusion that others are doing something bad, which I'm sure there will be plenty of bad behaviour. However, I don't think we can assume as much as we sometimes do at times.

#🎫︱general-support 10:00:31 dirvine.
Yeah, humanity's interesting, isn't it? There will always be people like that. The thing is, and the sad thing is, I think when you're involved with these projects you're desperate, absolutely desperate for critique, but proper thought-out critique. As soon as it gets near slander, then I think that's one thing about here we could just ban them. The forum's the same, people can just be timed out or stopped, but you really want active, progressive debate. It's a fine line, and I don't think anybody's ever got it right.
💯 2 👍 2
View in Discord →
#🎫︱general-support 10:05:22 dirvine.
I also think people, including myself, perhaps tend to think that governments, corporations, businesses, or open source maintainers are a finely tuned machine that are making decisions as one, when in fact all of them are broken to various degrees. So we tend to analise every decision, whether it's good or bad, and we associate that with a single entity, when in fact it's loose groups of people who are making the decisions. Governments can't even fix potholes in the road, but yet we assume that they're following some grand plan to overtake society. However, while all that's going on and there's all these theories left, right, up, down, or whatever, there is a technology. It is happening. It's the advancement of knowledge in AI, robotics, and everything. And there will be people, maybe not governments, maybe not even organisations, but there will be people that will be able to wield power and be making unilateral decisions. That's what concerns me much more than any project or government or open source maintainer. It's the what's coming. And what's coming could be amazing, absolutely amazing, or it could be quite dreadful. It will be in between both of those things, probably, like most things are. However, it's the thing I think we should all be quite concerned with: the realisation that most of the current debates that we have in society are futile, and the binary positions that people have are also futile. But there is actually some serious work to do right in front of us.
💪 3
View in Discord →
#🎫︱general-support 10:06:44 dirvine.
I think secure private networks, secure private communications are going to be critical elements of what's required. And they're going to have to be completely secure, completely peer-to-peer, and completely able to work in the form of small mesh networks that can be highly partitioned and then also rejoin into larger networks. That's a big part of what I'm working on now: looking at what happens in the event of a collapse of some kind.

I'm not talking about anyone in particular, but I just wonder as AI comes along: does it help if filtering is much more intelligent? You would hope it would be much more intelligent filtering, so that we can get that balance of debate, improvement, innovation, and forward momentum for society. It's an interesting canundrum.

#🌐︱general-chat 20:35:02 dirvine.
We want different here, but if folk all herd around cloud providers to run nodes then this is what will happen. So we need to continue to work very hard on decentralising well beyond any single player / supplier. I am sure we will though as there is a will to do so. What these AWS outages show though for sure is suprising effects in apparently secure or decentralised systems, I hear even signal was affected to. That's all suprising, but if folk can "get something" from running on some provider then these risks will remain.

📅 Sunday, 2025-10-26
#🌐︱general-chat 18:48:06 dirvine.
It does have mobile capabilities, but we are also providing swift and kotlin API's. Tauri v2 has a couple of small release issues right now (tauri build needs to be updated to 2.9 where it's only 2.5 and tauri is at 2.9, we don't control that). So to be clear tauri is able to run on mobile, but it's not write once run everywhere at all, you do need to repliment the front end for mobile, so you need a frontend for each plartofrm and use tauri to wrap it, otherwise you jsut go for it and use swift and kotlin. Not a simple choice, but it is a choice.

❤️ 2
View in Discord →
#🎫︱general-support 10:29:41 dirvine.
For CRDT, there is no real wrong copy. There are older copies, and they are always valuable until you get a newer copy. I'm not sure all of our clients are following the CRDT methodology correctly. However, what should be happening is you get as many copies as you possibly can. You use the latest copy, so the highest vector clock, but also you should publish that latest copy back to everybody that gave you an older copy.
👍 2
View in Discord →
#🎫︱general-support 18:25:08 dirvine.
> I also think we can't trust clients, only nodes. Clients don't have enough skin in the game. Maybe clients/nodes are being used interchangeably above though? This is correct but what we're talking about here is data on this network, so it's either immutable and self-referential. Or it's signed by the owner. So it's not like we're trusting clients here in any way where they can create new data or whatever. It's not about trust, we've sort of removed the trust element here. So data is either signed by the owner (and correct) or it's self-referential (and correct). Anything else is incorrect and would not be stored. It's these subtle areas where I think the scratchpad could be really abused though. The scratchpad intention was literally a place for apps to write config data or other unstructured data, but I think, maybe it's correct and people are following the rules, scratchpads could be really stretched out to hold actual information. Actually, this is not secure and isn't following CRDT patterns, etc. I try and keep my eye on that, but it's not straightforward. It's not really up to us to force a particular use of an API. If the API is here, folks should be able to use it whatever way they possibly feel they can. If we really don't want something to happen, it shouldn't be in our API to have the ability to do that. Scratchpads are definitely a grey area, where there's grey, there's probably confusion. It's really difficult because it's that mix of trying to do something that's a little bit more flexible, and then people take that flexibility and push it to the absolute extreme. I'm not saying anybody's right or anybody's wrong, I'm just noting that.
👍 4
View in Discord →
#🎫︱general-support 19:06:17 traktion0_20257 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
> I also think we can't trust clients, only nodes. Clients don't have enough skin in the game. Maybe ... [truncated]
Right, understood. So, it's more that clients can advertise their signed mutable data, to make others aware of it. They can't change it, as the signature would be invalidated. 👍

📅 Wednesday, 2025-10-29
#🎫︱general-support 11:42:01 dirvine.
This conversation above referenced chunks, scratch pads and pointers, mutable and immutable data. Scratch pads and pointers are different items. Same as chunks are different. The ability to misuse should not be characterised in any negative way from what I've said. When people design an API, they design it for a particular use, and they hope that it meets that use. The better the API, the harder it is to use, different from what was anticipated. It's not black and white, it's not binary. Love, hate. API design is a very tricky thing, and the more flexibility you try and provide with an API, the more use cases it can be used for, obviously. Sometimes those use cases are unintended; sometimes that could be a brilliant thing; and sometimes it could be a troublesome thing.

#🎫︱general-support 19:33:03 dirvine.
The network was originally envisaged to be apps running nodes. So everyones computer was a node and they all connected in a large network. Life moved on, mobiles happened, cloud happened and many other things. Anyway we ended up with node runners that means folks not running apps, but just running nodes. Some folk running nodes for pure profit will do any amount of harm to others or the network to make a single $0.000001 for themselves. This is the. game we are currently in. It is how it is, people will take everything and leave nothing, given the chance. Not all peopel by any means, but we are the special species that will harm each other cause we can 😉 However we need to consider this sort of thing more, emissions are a tool to help node runners as we get off the ground, that is takign longer than we wanted, but great progress is being made. I think we will be pulled to pieces until we are off the ground, jsut as the bitcoin network would have at teh start, had more folk acted in the extreme greed mode, but the original bitcoin crowd were very different to the new crows in these projects now, that's just how it is. I think this is great to report @Dimitar and I hope it's not cost you personally. I don't totally blame that guy, but I am not suprised, the loud accusing voices tend to be the very folks gaming the system as he was. They get a lot of ego boosting from sidelining us and fake reporting other stuff etc. It's also why I get annoyed when we see folk screaming about whales and other folk getting rewards, there is often another reason behind that kind of screaming and shouting. It's another thing we need to take care off as folk report stuff. They are not always honest 😉 (our community has tended to believe most folk and take them at face value, I am not so inclined to do that any more) Anyway, it's something to fix for sure, the nodes not behaving for sure need to be cleared out of the network quickly. It is an area where staking does help where bad actors lose stakes. Kinda sad, but true.
❤️ 2
View in Discord →

#🎫︱general-support 20:27:18 dirvine.
I think we need to consider everything. I Was watching the Solana guy earlier and interesting, bitcoin, eth, solana all went through hell and errors in "getting there". So it's normal, we have faired quite well, but we will get many more scammers and bammers like that dude. I kinda think between the lines we already do have a few in the community and I do see them winding folk up into frenzies and doing so easily as it's always easy to blame "the man" and in our case many times autonomi is seen as "the man" when it suits. I am relaxed though as underneath a lot is happeing. i.e. misbehaving nodes are currently handled in many ways, perhaps not all. But the way they are handled is quite unusual and pretty cool in my mind. What will happen is they look normal, their routing tables fill up as normal, but they don't appear in other nodes routing tables. So they will earn zero or closer to zero than normal nodes. There's a few other things happen as well, but we don't speak of them all, all the time. Then of course folk freak out wanting to know everything, but the scammers and bammers are watching, waiting on any small split in the skirt. So we balance. Then we work on making nodes as small as possible to run on tiny cheap PC's the counter to that is folk run fekin thousands of them on cloud servers. So we pay for helping the poorer with a repayment fo folk centralising our decentralised network. The balance is hard for sure, every good thing can be mirrored in a bad outcome. So we move on learning and finding out more, but like I said watching the solana guy and hearing their story, we are actually quite lucky. But like them we do not put out our 100% complete adn 100% secure never needing to update system, but folk are quick to point out we are not 100% complete in every way and try and lynch us for that too, but that's jsut more nonsense you have to take on board. So the trick is, stay cool, stay focussed and keep on moving forward. Realise those who can cause harm, will and those who want you to succeed will have little patience and even less compassion, in many cases and keep going. So far we have been very lucky with our wee gang, but some have fallen foul to the holier than though high positioned preacher position, but most have stayed true and kept us moving forward. So the key is to remain passionate, but not get emotional and that is hard as passion is likely an emotion 😄 😄 Balance those apples 😄 😄
❤️ 9 💪 3
