#🎫︱general-support 21:16:35 dirvine.
Of course, that is clear. However if we say We are working on a fix for X Scammer says, Oh wow I never even knew X was an issue and I never knew how to exploit that. So until the fix for X is in place I am gonna scam the fek out of the network using X and get as much as I can. Now we are launched and a larger community we simply cannot upfront speak of X. It's like the nrmal process of zero day's where folk find the issue and alert the team who fix it then tell folk it's fixed and publish CVE etc. I am ready for the many folk saying we should discuss everything up front, but it's not sensible, clever or helpful. I am sure many will see it different, but that is how the industry works and for good reason. Trust in the open community is not a clever way forward with such issues.

📅 Tuesday, 2025-11-04
#🌐︱general-chat 08:07:09 dirvine.
This is really superb, the bones and more alrady in place. The polish happening (but it will never be polished fast enough for many 😄 ) Don't lose heart with the impatience of the folk who are desperate for immediate gratification and 100% complete 100% polished 100% the most user friendly totally bug free product that never needs any more work and is like no other software. Sent from God totally complete does not exist 😄 However the fast delivery, actualy use and speed of delivery is amazing. The showcase fo autonomi backend is perfect and the speed of delivery is outstanding. Now with @WhiteOutMashups back and the folks already building we will see at some point the crossing of the chasm to the non autonomi non p2p non technical userbase. That userbase is Everyone and that is the goal. This is a stepping stone and we all need to applaud all stepping stones and encourage this type of work. I plead with everyone to feedback carefully and thoughtfully, don't flood folks with a zillion personal comments. We all need to think "What can we personally do that just helps these projects" What I mean is think like this - What action can I take to help - What action can I take that requires the developer DO NOTHING (do not suck energy from them) - perhaps even saying well done, with zero sting in the tail (you know after the `but,`) Then as much as humanly possible do these actions. It may be too early for some things, like shouting from rooftops or "marketing" (yes that bullet that is immeasurable and alway done wrong in many opinions) So let's ALL HELP these projects, feedback useful issues and bugs and most importantly work out how to help, @southside show us ways here, @Dimitar quietly pushes things forward and both do at their own cost in many ways, mostly time. Anyway @cobite. (safemedia) hats off to you!!! excellent vision

📅 Thursday, 2025-11-06
#🌐︱general-chat 11:45:02 dirvine.
GraphEntry is efectivly a list of items pointed ot by their parent. You can have a single pointer point to the last entry and update that poiner if you add more entries in the "list". So a graphentry allows you of have history of "stuff" you can traverse back in time. The pointer can be used to "point" to the relevent entry, so latest, significant event etc.
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 11:55:33 dirvine.
I have read most of the concerns and opinions. I have no real god like opinion. However many ideas would be us uploading dat to the network, which currently means paying a ton of fees to Eth, which we are workgin on solving. But that is separate. Then others say just stop, others say don't and others say something else. Mostly I see much more emotion than clear thought. It's a very sensitive and highly opinionated thing. As per the whitepapers, emissions will be paid out for a very long time and they are to build the network. So then folk may argue, build the size, build the capability, build .... you name it. So I have read almost everything, most of the team have, but honestly in terms of what we focus on, we take account of everything. So it's something for sure, it can be improved for sure and we are looking at it alongside the community for sure. What we don't want is knee jerk or any emotional take. We want to always jsut do what is best for the network and a very small % of what this network is, is emissions. So it sits there

#🌐︱general-chat 12:02:52 mightyfool0689 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
I have read most of the concerns and opinions. I have no real god like opinion. However many ideas w ... [truncated]
Very good to hear that the team has read the concerns and are seeing it can be improved. I also agree that the discussion is probably lead by emotions or at the very least started by emotions. I know the declining price sure made me look a second time at how emissions work. What stands out for me is that I'm not exactly sure what emission is trying to accomplish. Node stability has been named before. What I fail to understand is why we'd want node stability at such a low network node capacity. And I know that we're in quite a weird place with eth fees being high when uploading and that should soon improve, but in a general sense I just don't think we should ever aim for having emission when node capacity is below a certain point that we aim for (I think that's the 50% mark?). If we'd tie emission to node storage usage, we'd now have 99% reduced emission. With nodes that are on the network actually using the resources they're providing instead of burning energy and resources for nothing and being subsidized in the process. It's money leaving the ecosystem for no good reason. Then again, I can see how this might be an issue here and now, but will no longer be an issue when ETH fees are reduced, paymaster is here and we have people uploading data.

I would ignore it all folks. Those are the kind of folk who glue razor blades in kids playgrounds. They are worth zero. 📅 Thursday, 2025-11-13
#🌐︱general-chat 21:23:26 dirvine.
Yes @chriso it's a ton of factors and I think it's good to consider as many as possible at any one time. - The network is still small - Data stored is still small - Protections from network being larger with more data are not in place (safety in numbers) There are a ton of things we need as time goes on, but many are now required to accelerate implementation due to the slower "take off". The reasons for the slow take off are also many. So for everyone, there are a lot of back end work being done in many areas, such as auto upgrades, data health monitoring, bad node detection and so on. Many of these will NEVER be complete, the network will NEVER be 100% secure, no network, vehicle, drug or product will ever be, so we need to always stay real. The team need to keep moving forward. However the network is doing amazing so far and this is the largest challenge we have had with data, we will find out how much an issue soon, but more importantly improve the resilience significantly and as I say this is a never ending thing we must do. However we do have some work we want in place in the next few weeks to ensure significant improvements in reliability and reliance. Much of that work will be quite technical and deep and also hard to explain, but we are also working on those explanations. We need to take care though, there are some who are attacking the network and getting kicks from that, with AI these folks are more dangerous, but also our ability to react is also much stronger. There is no single reason for the current hassle, it will be a mix of many. I think some folk will say it's emissions and that is not correct at all, that is almost certain. I hope very much that folk who take binary views on such issues consider that if they cannot list the pro's and con's of any of these things, then they are likely biased in their view. It's powerful to look both ways in all things in such a complex network. Due to attacks though we do need to be much more careful what we say, we cannot naval gaze in public any more, we need to consider we are looking after real people in a real world now and we must secure our world. We cannot fuel attackers with hints and tips for them to get their kicks while the team work hard with blood sweat and tears getting us there. However we are also doing a ton on comms to ensure we get better comms and a closer more tight knit community as we go. No easy balance, but we are determined and we do have grit. So we will move onwards and we can all be certain of that. So we will get to the bottom of this and we will remedy what we can and prevent any bugs we find from biting us in the future.

#🌐︱general-chat 23:01:30 dirvine.
The foundation was set up with a ton of legal work to facilitate the contracts given to the lawyers. That included emissions etc. To design some new mechanism for emissions is far from trivial. Setting up new legal contracts for emissions and the tax position of the foundation is non trivial. None of this is trivial, none of the team are stating "facts" about levels of emmissions and their affects, we are not that smart at crstal ball stuff, but we are very very busy getting the network up and running as it shoudl be so that folk can upload real data in real apps to complete teh path we set out. It's takign longer, folk are understandably wanting it all faster, especially us, but racing around like dogems in a lightning storm wont help us here. There is a path, we are on that path, we need to get the work done we have in the pipeline. Opinions are great, reading folk making absolute statements is entertaining and interesting, but none of us know the effects of all these things like emmissions, sure memes will happen, opinions will solidify and become fact, but it does not change the work we must do.

📅 Saturday, 2025-11-15
#🌐︱general-chat 14:42:47 dirvine.
Must be bad coms, but honestly I see a lot fo things start as option and then become fact. It's a bad look to always defend as we saying we did not do X we do not want Y and so on. So the whitepaper is there, it's being executed. The foundation is there handling legal side of execution of the whitepaper and validated by all the legals (which is a nightmare and Swiss law is great, but very very in depth and we have to validate each change at a significant cost etc.). In terms of other core teams doing work in the backend, then this is what we want, but maidsafe so far are the only team with deep knowledge, not driven by short term emotinoal changes and so on. There is a lot of work for maidsafe to have to prove to the foundation they are doing what the whitepaper and legals state. These action include ensuring nobody from the foundation can benefit and so on. Its quite strict. So if there are other team who can work to the whitepaper and can show they will do at least what maidsafe does, including the level of devops and testnets and measurements etc. then it's what we want to see. It's significant backend work though for sure, but our future surely has to be totally decentralised development. Finding that mechanism and those teams is a goal for certain 100% it's a goal. The foundation though should be seen as the human element we currently use, but their goal is to also become irrelevant as we automate away any authority they have over time. Smart contracts are a huge help, but not enough, however we have a way to go even there to pass more human decisions to smart contracts. Emissions is interesting as we could have smart contracted that away and then no opinion woudl matter to change that. So defo ying/yang vibes there and this is what we need to be very careful off. We cannot totally automate just yet and we cannot bend to the whim of only small groups, no matter how loud they shout, but we cannot either ignore good ideas and progressive changes over time. Anyway, the granite like approach to following any agreed plans is vital, then we have still humans in the loop, we have the ability to change, it's very non trivial to do so though and this is where we are. What is good with the foundation though is the folk there cannot benefit and they make decisions only for the good of the network, not for any group or philosophy etc. They will also not directly have a channel for folk to debate with, they will follow the agreed route that is signed up for and make changes when they must, but at a cost and a timescale for sure. If we got the white-paper wrong as a community and company then there will be routes to change, but currently the goal is network stability and everyone may have an opionion, but the core tem or another core team need to be focussed and consider deeply all aspects and then apply to the foundation for funding to continue their work.

#🌐︱general-chat 09:42:42 dirvine.
Like much it's not simple. Here's current techincal issues. Kademlia uses "buckets" of nodes (addresses) to cover the whole network. So you have 256 buckets in a routing table. Each bucket in our implementation can hold a max of 20 nodes Bucket 255 has 20 nodes and represents 50% of total network Bucket 254 has 20 nodes and represents25% of total network All the way to bucket 0 which will never fill (this is your closest bucket) Continuing ---
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 09:45:57 dirvine.
So we can say have the K (bucket size i.e. 20) closest nodes you know of are your close group in KAD. This 20 nodes will NOT be in total agreement with al 20 nodes, but as close as they can get. So we use a subset of the actual K to get a more accurate answer (it's math measurable to some extent) and we have 5 (arguably 7 in fact) The larger we make the autonomi CLOSE_GROUP (5) then the less accurate that it. The kademlia bucket size (20) would be a max here.
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 09:48:43 dirvine.
So you can increate kademlia K to say 36 or 64 etc. but that increases the size of the routing table and therefor resourse used. So it's costly. However we also need to consider time and tech. So whereas 20 years ago K == 20 was too big and folk used 8, nowadaya both memory and bandwith are much more cheap, so there is chances to increase kad K and therefor Autonomi CLOSE_GROUP. There are many other considerations, but these are the. most basic of them. It's a few parameters and we need to consider them all together in terms of resource costs, and also resource capability of machines we want to run on as well as advances in memory/cpu and bandwidth
👍 3 🤯 1
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 09:50:49 dimitarsafenetworkbulgaria → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
So you can increate kademlia K to say 36 or 64 etc. but that increases the size of the routing table ... [truncated]
This is beyond my ability to understand. Do I understand correctly that 5 is the maximum and if we want more security the solution cannot be at the protocol level, but at the software level - for example, in Dave, add the ability to set from 1 to 100 copies to be generated and uploaded from the specified file/folder and have 1-100 different data addresses?
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 09:54:57 dirvine.
Yes, this is true, also caching and churn can help. So when you are responsibel for a record and them move away as yo are no longer closest, you keep (cache) that data until you cannot hold it any longer as you are responsible for records and you need the space and so on. There is then also deterministic caching (positively expand the replicant copies which helps a ton in high churn. Also opportunistic caching, which is less efficient and ndoes just cache what they can see in an LRU (least recently used) cache so nodes use exatra space to store stuff they are not responsible for, but in an allocated space they use for this where they can and don't need that space. This requires us to have a gossip type layer to allow the network to broadcast a lost record message and any node on the network will see that and reply with the data. So there is a ton of stuff we have in our armoury here, most of it is invisible work though and not simple, but all very possible. The community will need to be informed of this as we move forward, but also give us time to do a ton of stuff we cannot easily communicate as it becomes too complex, but also we need to be super careful of attackers as well these days. It is all super interesting for me but boring as hell for most folk, which is cool and normal.
❤️ 8 👍 2 🤯 1
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 09:58:16 dirvine.
No it is a protocol level thing, but it's not a simple button to just change at will, the ramifications are not straighforward. Think of the code as a nuclear power station with a hundred big red dials. We need to understand all the dials and how they interoperate. So it's hard, but not impossible to alter parameters, but almost none are detached from all teh rest and each dial alters the whole network and affects other dials. Bottom line, replication count is changable, but it's beyond what we can detail in a few on line messages really. But it is posisble with great care

#🌐︱general-chat 10:05:53 dirvine.
AI really helps @Dimitar when we have a load of such parameters. Given our code base and not asking for code changes (as it may change code, which we don't want) but then get into long conversations about why we have a K lower than kad K and how can we change that while not increasing machine respurce use or what level of resource use discounts what % of machines capable of runnign nodes and so on. Then you can get into the social aspects of it all and so on. In any case changing K would have been months of a seniour level tech team a few years ago and now you can get a very long way with a single senior dev and AI debating it all. AI still takes it too simply, but a few well thought out prompts to tell it to not forget certain important aspects (the still hard part to know) and you can get really far. It's why normal folk do an AI thing and post it to us and say look and we ignore that, it still takes a serious amount of domain knowledge, but it's certainly much more tractable for the normal dude these days, with great care though. Don't let is persuade you you are a genious and have solved a huge issue, it keeps telling me that and it's wrong, but with the correct use and pushback and domain knowledge then it is super powerful.

#🌐︱general-chat 10:25:55 dirvine.
The network does not work like that, but if nodes note a node being bad they ignore him. The ignored node will not get data to store and no payments eventually, but will get limited payment while being effectively killed. There is no God like creature here, it is very much like an ant colony, there is no leader, good nodes work and reject bad nodes So there may be folk not getting rewards over time and that is hwo it will work, but we also need to ensure good nodes do get paid etc. So we nede to be careful when folk are sayign they get no rewards and they need fixed and so on. It's not simple 😄 😄

📅 Tuesday, 2025-11-18
#🌐︱general-chat 09:29:22 dirvine.
It's an interesting angle, a very secure persistent data store, but not the only one. It is, though, one that is accessible by all, at all times, without any third party involvement. For incredibly long storage of data that is persistent over time, then we still also have drives, like worm drives and, in future, many ways to store data over great lengths of time, a bit like the back shelves in a large library that people may take documents they consider highly, highly sensitive (something like the Declaration of Independence or the Bible or whatever), and also have copies of it off the network. So it is an interesting angle where we don't try to be everything to everybody all the time, but we are a persistent, perpetual data storage that is critical to humanity, but we're not the only persistent data storage humanity will have available to it. AI, for instance, or large language models are quite interesting at compressed data. It's like the modern version of Encyclopaedia Britannica or a better version of Wikipedia than we previously had. A significant amount of general knowledge, how to create fire, how to cook food, how to hunt animals, how to build machines, how to do code, is contained within them. That is a massive part of humanity's data compressed into a very small space. But again, it's not everything for everyone all the time, but it's an interesting angle that in a few gigabytes you can have what previously would have been many petabytes of data, just to get general knowledge. Then you have, of course, as I mentioned, the highly specialised documents, the ones that we want to preserve almost exactly as they were written, without any change or interpretation. And people may want to have many copies of them in many places. But current commercial information or current knowledge, documentation for businesses, for apps, for app developers, for individuals, for group sharing, then we have this network and it's got a really good place in our world. It stands in a very unique position, running on consumer level devices where everybody should be able to run a node anywhere on the computer that they're running their app on anyway, and using their spare capacity for this network. So there's a lot to it, but it's an interesting angle.
👍 1
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 10:08:56 dirvine.
We can argue small networks are better than large networks, no rewards are better than tons of them and all in between 😄 The beauty of humanity and the mastery of hindsight will always be in place. A thing I am intregued by is teh common approach of making complex Pareto front just a binary issue on just one measure. I am also optimistic and 100% convinced when we look back we will be able to say, what if ... and add in there many suggestions, but we will almost never know. So many variables. I appreciate your nod to the team knowing stuff as many times these things are them and us, where the team are portrayed as clueless and the community as oracles, when in fact we are all probably about the same level of intelligence and some will be highly economy motivated, some marketing, some deep tech and so on. However together all these "competing" silo's of knowledge end up bing the whole project. The dev team are just that, the dev team, the economic side, we have folk who are fekin incredible in there and also in the community, marketing likewise, but all in all it's the mix of all these things that matter, there is no binary issue that solves all or causes all ill etc. It's just easy to find an enemy and much harder to find the truth at times.

#🎫︱general-support 13:11:31 dirvine.
A bit like bitcoin needed many miners well before transactions were a thing, autonomi needs nodes before data becomes a thing. It's a very similar thing in many ways. Illogical for sure, stupid as called by many people, but data will flow, but infrastructure has to be there, plentiful, safe and secure before that. So what may seem illogical to many is actually provably what worked in the past. There is debate about how many and which way to get these nodes running and so forth and these debates will last until we get traction, much like bitcoin was shunned and scoffed at by many "intellectuals". It's daft till it works 😄 😄

#🎫︱general-support 13:32:07 dirvine.
I think this is a confusion. Bitcoin had two mining pools which owned more than 51% for many, many years. They may still actually do that. At that level, what you do is when miners or node operators are running and they need to protect data or they need to protect transactions, they fight each other. But we're trying for something different from what Bitcoin did with the rewards, and we're trying to distribute things a lot more evenly in smaller amounts. The fact that you have large mining pools, the way autonomi is running, there may be large providers of storage. Our hope is eventually that it becomes so simple to run a node that individuals are running them. At the moment, we've got a fight between folk running lots of nodes and folk who want to run lots of nodes. The incentives at the moment are good that they achieve some things, bad that they promote other things. However, there are always incentives. If there's no incentive for anything in life, that thing doesn't happen. Everything has an incentive, it's just you have to work out what is the incentive and what is it you're trying to get from it. If you have an incentive that you want many nodes running, that's one thing. And then if you want another incentive, you want to ensure that the nodes that are running are storing data and behaving properly, that's a different incentive with different sets of rules. These things develop over time, but you have to develop it over time.
👍 1
View in Discord →
#🎫︱general-support 13:40:01 dirvine.
I'm sorry you feel that way, it's not true, but I am sorry you feel that way. I don't know what to do about it. The code is open source. Anybody can do what they want. They could create a competing team. They could try and make the autonomi network different and better. There's lots of ways to do something, but I think somebody quite famously said a while ago: activism and commenting and whatnot doesn't change anything. Actions do. So people should act and try and do something different if they feel that the team, whether that's Maidsafe or Autonom, the network, foundation, but people feel the team aren't doing something and they could do it better. There's absolutely nothing to stop anyone doing it better. This is what it's all about. It's decentralised, permissionless, open source. There's no need for really activism, there's plenty of opportunity for action.

#🎫︱general-support 13:41:15 dirvine.
The only levers we hold is that we control our own destiny, and we accept and appreciate that everyone else controls their own destiny. The tools and the work that we do is to provide people the opportunity to create their own destiny, so people can join us and help, or do it differently.
View in Discord →
#🎫︱general-support 13:52:37 dirvine.
No worries chap, but if you find a governance model that works, you should call the United Nations and then wait for your Nobel Peace Prize. Seriously though, governance is such an issue that so many people shout for. We want better governance, a change in governance. Worldwide, every project, every open source project, everywhere. People want better governance, but nobody actually has got a really good governance model. If we did get a great governance model, we literally would win a Nobel Peace Prize. Famine would be over, war would be over. Think people think exist that doesn't exist, we have to do our best, we have to take feedback, we have to listen to opinion, and we do listen to opinion. But we very much appreciate that some people's opinion is very strong in some direction. There's not much we can do about that, but we do have to make some decisions to get any forward progress. People can live with the decisions we make and continue, or they could do, as you say, take time out or find another project that's more in line with their own governance or strategy or whatever. It's really not an issue. It should never be personal and it should never be some great big shouting match. I appreciate the fact that you're not making it a shouting match.

As I say, it's something we are looking at, but it's really not straightforward. What we get from it is difficult. We have to figure out what we would be trying to measure from such a network.

#🎫︱general-support 14:27:09 dirvine.
That's the issue, it would be a significant delay, I mean really significant, but also what do we get out of it? If we've got people running nodes and doing it properly, we don't actually get much out of it. We really need the folk who are scamming us and vandalising the network. You really need them to get a lot of information about the nodes. Where test nets actually help an awful lot is the client developer side, where people can develop things, see if they work well, and then put them onto the main net.

📅 Thursday, 2025-11-27
#🌐︱general-chat 16:44:41 dirvine.
I think it's fine to defend against bullying, condescension, and lying when it's a team forum like this. It's not a public forum for anybody for any reason. If it was, then full free open speech, 4chan and all that. But here it's the team trying to engage with the community. I think the rules are fine, and if people want to engage with the team, the bare minimum that they should be able to request is that they treat the team with respect and they demand respect from the team. I think that's not unreasonable. So it's not a free speech issue, it's not a free speech system, it's basically the team and their own Discord to communicate with the community and, most importantly, to get critique and feedback and try and see if we can just make this a better network, which is ultimately what we all want. With that I'm never gonna stand by and let people be bullied, or lied to, or spoken down to. Certainly not in a forum that has got a specific purpose like this one.

📅 Sunday, 2025-11-30
#🌐︱general-chat 10:13:28 dirvine.
Small team, big job, impatient community (rightly so and good in many ways). All sharing the same vision, can't be too bad 😉 The world is about to change in many strange ways, economies are about to be challenged, politics is as crazy as ever, people are scared and unable to tell exactly what is coming. Longevity, povery, discovery, emptiness, nobody knows. However, we do know that people need agency, they need security, they need privacy, they need their own space, they need protection and much much more. In these uncertain times it will take an effort, that effort will require support, we have both in spades and we are all doing this, Chris more than most, the team are very focussed, our CEO is laser focussed and acutely aware of the importance of this project and it's impact on the world, she keeps reminding me and the team of that. We will win, if we consider winning as giving it our best shot, if we consider it as trying like hell to do the right thing and if we consider as our duty to not just the community, but to everyone on this planet. We have been the unstoppable project, we have a powerful vision and we will keep on doing this tough job, not cause it's easy for sure 😄 but because we think every person should do at least their bit, even when the screaming, shouting and lecturing happens around us, we keep on going and I love it!! We have to do it.
❤️ 24 🐉 7 :Autonomi: 8 💯 6 💪 2 :David: 1

📅 Monday, 2025-12-15
#🌐︱general-chat 10:59:15 dirvine.
There will be a day when software crytographic attestation works. That will be explosive and will mean nodes can confirm other nodes are running exactly the code they should. No more no less. At that time, our network, software and code is completely secure. In that space, native token or ANY software can convey any digital asset from node to node, form person to person wtih 100% integrity. No need for proof of work or stake, no blockchian, no smart contract, just pure simple software defined simple rules. When that happens and there is an upgrade all nodes will have to upgrade to the current state as all nodes will be runnign pure unadulterated code, no change at all will be accepted by the network. At that time everyone wins, no cheating, no scammers, no spammer and no thieves. No undprovisioning, no data deleting, no 3rd party data stores or fake nodes. No groups of nodes from the same IP, or provider, no data held on less than 5 geographic regions, no cheating of any kind. That will mean all nodes have to upgrade together, it's an apparently impossible thing to have pure software attestation, but that's what we are here for is it not? In any case, pure decentralisation will require consistent and coherent code and environments and it will require this is provably coherent. It's not gonna be possible to be decentralised and all nodes running any code in any manner, when you think of it? There's a decentalisaiton dilemma, not many think of, but one we all need to be aware of IMO
👍 5 ❤️ 5

I don't think folk are getting this. I am saying with attestation we can prove a exact function was ... [truncated]
If I understand correctly, you're suggesting the goal would be to know the exact same code is always run. Same function, same machine code, guaranteed. Is that correct? If so, I'd argue that is both good and bad. If you _only_ want to trust the vendor of the code and be sure that it is their code you are executing, that's great. However, what if you _don't_ want to trust a single vendor? What if you want to encourage different implementations, using different languages, from different teams? What if the preference is to have _competition_ to avoid vendor lock-in/monopoly? If I'm misunderstanding, please disregard the above. However, it sounds very much like needing to trust a specific version of software, by a specific vendor (or trusted list of software/vendors, etc).

If you only want to trust the vendor of the code This is the infuriating part for me. If the code was - you can spend what you have, but only once It's a single function and that function represents almost all of a digital currency. All the other code you speak of, the tons of other stuff in our codebases are to make that single thing work. Pure attestation is not about trusting the code, it's about having super simple agreed functions in code without all the bloat and crypto stuff to make the functions folk promise to actually work. If you think not code, but simple functions that are provably run then the complexity of modern code is gone, scammers re gone, hackign is gone etc. et.c Trust the vendor becomes more trust the simple agreed upon function the code does. I honestly believe folk want complexity hackers and spammers as somehow that is freedom 🙃 Honestly folks, I am just confused, no slander or anything but I am lost and away back to my cave 😄 😄

#🌐︱general-chat 14:05:08 traktion0_20257 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
>If I understand correctly, you're suggesting the goal would be to know the exact same code is alway ... [truncated]
If it helps to explain my point, I'd want to trust the binary for my banking app, where the vendor is the bank. Clearly, I already have a trust relationship with them. If the aim is a trustless, distributed, data network, I don't want to be trusting any binaries from _any_ vendor. I want there to be competition in node development teams. I want there to be agreement on the protocol through a majority of nodes agreeing. Afaik, that's the goal for Autonomi nodes already. I have no intention of inviting scammers/hackers or whatever. Don't let me keep you from your cave though 😅
👍 3
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 15:06:39 dirvine.
There is absolutely confusion here. This is not just about trusting binaries. however there is trust in those who create code, that's for sure. The mix you have is do you want to allow some scammers, but not too many and do you not want to trust any team, but allow all teams (no code signing etc.) and then encourage folk to download from anywhere? Attestation is a mechanism where any supplier can safely say this code is correct, the node I am speaking to is also correct. Otherwise it is a majority decision where some folk are allowed to be incorrect, or scammers and there is no trust at all. I just don't think the notion of 100% untrusted code on an untrusted network is valid, somebody has to trust somebody somewhere, otherwise it's a free for all with all kinds of code. Majority is a good and solid way of trying to ensure code is correct, but attestation is a way of knowing code is correct. I am not sure the trustless thing is at all real (it's a nice meme), we don't see folk safely downloading bitcoin nodes or wallets from any random place, there does have to be an element of trust. That generally means you trust the code does what it says, not because you can prove it, but because the supplier says so and you trust them. What I am talking about is "knowing" the code does what it says, even when it's run on other folk computers. A bit like deterministic builds, but of simple functions and not masses of complex code It's not about binaries, we all run binaries, it's more like signing if you like, where signing proves the source. Here attestation also guarantees the function of the code. It does not stop many teams creating code and competing either, but it would force teams to collaborate and allow each to attest each others code is functionally correct. It's been impossible for sure and likely still is, who knows?
👍 4
View in Discord →

#🌐︱general-chat 12:12:14 dimitarsafenetworkbulgaria → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
I think you can now share notebooklm sessions @Dimitar I feel that way folk see the sources, can add ... [truncated]
The source material is a script for the video I provided. I then review the finished video to check for any obvious inaccuracies. Overall, I am very satisfied with the result. If I were to go through it manually, frame by frame, I might change one or two words, but nothing more. The main idea is to create a series of videos that present the network based on the values and perspective of the viewer — for example, from the point of view of a small business owner, supporters of green politics, and so on. Essentially, for any audience you want to address, there will be a tailored way to present the network using the right tool.

#🌐︱general-chat 13:24:46 dirvine.
At exponentially increasing rates, which is incredible to all. More folk to see and importantly know what is happening and also a view in the Engineering (which is ultimately the application of science which is a myriad of tradeoffs). Folk love or hate AI, but for me I am super bullish it levels the playing field and if that means giving everyone access to more knowledge I am all in. Incumbent "experts" and old coders will fight this, but it's pointless. I think of it as encyclopaedia Britanica version 0.1, when it gets to v1.0 then we all win. Currently I would say it's 0.8). Others fear the take over of big tech, but that is also false IMO as no matter where it goes, we all already have a crap ton more knowledge at our hands to take it further ourselves. This is rapidly becoming more true as time goes on and if we had a decentralised secure borderless permissionless network, then we can share that knowledge and nobody can stop us. The same will happen with robots, we will all win, folk will try and succeed in taking control, but only for a wee while.

#🌐︱general-chat 16:50:41 dirvine.
Saorsa is a 7 layer network that does have 2 DHT's actually (ip4 and 6) as well as geo protections, greedy routing (small world hyperbolic) as well as some other stuff, but also potentially software attestation. Lot's of research but all relevant for a decentralised future for sure. saorsa is about 24 (or 25 including ant-quic) libraries right now. It's a lot of angles on all possible routes

📅 Wednesday, 2025-12-24
#🌐︱general-chat 11:41:08 dirvine.
It's like if bitcoin started again (it would fail), where you have incentive (in early btc days folk did not believe there was an incentive) you have outcomes. Nowadays any crypto currency thing is a huge magnet for the worst of us. No matter the damage to others folk will do what they can to steal or mock or just damage. It's our job to get above that, to let the good in humanity prevail, but the sheer amount of badness that some have means we have to fight hard to prevail, both technically and as a community. This is a classic of stay together and win or don't. So we need a strong community and strong technology, The devs can do what they can with the tech and we as a community need to do what we can to support that. Many will distract the devs on purpose, or try and get their time to themselves, selfishly or just want to demotivate them and call them names, but as a community we need to motivate, support and encourage. All this while all of us, devs and community have to fight a bigger battle against greed, hatred, malice and vandalism. It's all about how much we want this to work and then folk can argue about politics, AI or whatever the current moral high ground is. But first we need to win and that means we all have a shoulder to the wheel and push together, even if some of us feel we should slow down or speed up or whatever, if we lose direction we are just weaker. I want us to be stronger

#🌐︱general-chat 12:25:40 dirvine.
It will mean you can store many chunks for the price of one (in terms of gas), but if storing single chunks there will be no difference. So think of it like you can batch a load of chunks and pay the gas fee once as opposed to per chunk. It's early release too as we test it out given the network conditions we are working on.

📅 Friday, 2025-12-26
#🌐︱general-chat 21:54:06 dirvine.
hey folks I wonder if you can help me run a small test https://saorsa-1.saorsalabs.com it should allow you to download a small test binary and run it. All being well you should see some outbound and inbound connections (depends who else is also connected at the time). There is also a MASQUE relay which we can use to mask source IP addresses (but this requires much more Engineering as it's wise to not hide service providers IP addresses to allow us to GEO separate them, but that's for another day) It's disabled in this test It allows us to test quite a lot - Full post quantum TLS - Native quic nat traversal - ip4/6 dual scoped connections Plus quite a bit more (https://saorsa-1.saorsalabs.com/#architecture) , I feel we are close to a definitive answer here, let's see. NOTE: this is a test binary on the quic library, it's not meant as a fully polished app in ANY way shape or form, it's data collection on networking connectivity only, so think quick dirty app to test networking, not an app for folk to use. I really appreciate any help here and we may need to do this a couple of times over the next few weeks, but it is more than worthwhile. Don't worry about some connections that report a fail, if there is a connect at all it's a pass, even though we may fail a nat or ip6 or whatever on screen, that just extra info for me at this point, but it's looking good. I just need lots of differnt configuraitons and connections to see what happens here.

#🌐︱general-chat 08:54:03 dirvine.
Yes, there's a few options there. We can read the data from existing nodes directly from discs. We also can have a hybrid client that can accept data from one network onto the other network and accept data as being paid if it exists on the autonomi network. So there's lots of different options. The thing that we're researching here is just a very capable network layer. The bigger option is a fully hybrid node; however, that gets a bit tricky.

#🌐︱general-chat 23:54:51 dirvine.
Hey folks, I am running a longer test now if anyone wants to join, https://saorsa-1.saorsalabs.com/ It's a TUI app and easy to run, if you leave it running for an hour or so then that's great, it will give us all the data we need. This test includes saorsa-gossip and gossips around boostrap cache files as a test. It also sets auto co-ordination to enhance nat traversal even further. You may see reach figures over 100% which seems weird, but it's just more nodes found us than we new of, but we will know of them soon enough 😉 Anyway if folk want a play then please di, I have made more versions of the binary available, including musl for folk with weird linux versions etc. or raspberry pi and all that kind if thing I will maybe make the dashboard public too, I am just checking any security but here I am pretty certain we are fine. It's purely a small connectivity test. I am going offline for a sleep, been a long few days 😄 😄 These may be fast and furious tests, but they are extremely worthwhile. If we can connect all computers together then we really are in an amazing place as a community and humanity.

#🌐︱general-chat 09:23:38 dirvine.
what I have seen is libp2p in go is pretty good, rust is a bit behind. They lost much of their funding, so it's a little bit up in the air. iroh who split away have rewritten libp2p with some good results and some good devs too. It's got a lot of promise. It is a different and incompatible network layer though, but that is a bridge we can cross in many ways. ant-quic is a similar approach but much more focussed on dual stack (4 and 6) and things like raw keys instead of x502 certificates (so more p2p) and iroh are heading that direction too, but also the quantum secure tls is a big issue. Any library that can give us true secure p2p connections would be a huge win for society though. Libp2p has had millions spent and many more devs than the whole maidsafe project and that is just the basic network layer. This industry has been terrible as p2p networks cannot evolve without a true network layer that simply works. We badly need that, nobody seems to notice and even fewer care. I see now Vitalek and folks saying we cnnot be decentralised withought a p2p base network layer, so folk are noticing at last. It has been the bane of my life for 20 odd years and a total letdown of humanity for over 35 years. It's time to fix that most basic of things, but it does mean fighting the centralisation movements who all are motivated to keep the client server, monitoring and controlling base layer. Nobody notices this and not many work on it as it's super super complex and absolutely of no obvious value, so those that work on this will see no thanks, no value and no understanding from the tech community, never mind the general public. So it's a thankless massive task that needs fixed.
👍 10

#🎫︱general-support 08:50:12 dirvine.
What I want to do is get the logs much better. So less noisy and much more clear. i.e. smaller and more focussed. Then people can (with some practise as it's not simple) get an AI model with decent context (so gemini 3 type) to point at the log AND point to the autonomi/ant-node dir in our source code. That way we are all debugging with actual factual data. As I say it's not totally simple, this AI stuff requires a ton of work to use properly (you cannot "vibe code" anything deep like the autonomi network) but when you have it setup and you can use it (which means you can manage the AI, you don't need to understand our code), then we can all be very well informed. We all win then and much less guessing, which would be wonderful all round, community and core team would learn so much and improve so much faster.

📅 Thursday, 2026-01-08
#🎙︱discussion-topics 15:58:56 dirvine.
Currently, they are different markets. That project is aimed more at radio links and similar systems. When you're on the internet, UDP and TCP are the protocols you require. If you need to route information and whatnot, it's fascinating to keep an eye on other networks like LoRa radio networks or serial link networks, but there are definitely packet size limitations to consider. There are ways to pull it all together, but it's almost like a dual protocol stack: 1. You would have a UDP route with QUIC 2. You would have a non-UDP route for that other project It's not trivial (for sure) because the packet structure and everything else is quite different.Who knows

#🌐︱general-chat 14:04:55 dirvine.
Yea, I get the doubts folk have, but the proof is real. So that's not an issue. Then the loathing for "big tech" and "corporate control" which I agree with. However it's like the Internet, it's mostly corporate control, but we all use it, however many of us use it to escape that very corporate control. Same with phones, food, cars, roads ..... AI is just the same in my mind. You use it to build, like you use a car to drive away into the wilds away from the city you don't like. So use the most powerful tools you can lay your hands on to build you vision. If folk agree with the vision they won't mind you get there on a bus, plane or car, just that you got there, otherwise it was never about the vision it was all about controlling the journey. I think many folk to try and control the journey, critique each turn, shout at every bend in the road and so on and I get that, but to me it's the vision that we need to be steadfast about. Me, I will use whatever by whoever to achieve the vision, as long as nobody gets hurt then it's a win. (I know extremist views wil say any penny you give to "the man" hurts somebody, but then I also step on ant's and bend grass when walking as well, I don't mean to and I could avoid it at great costs and never get to my destination but I choose to travel wisely, so ho hum 😉 )

It takes a significant effort to get it right, but even a few hours shows what these can do. I hope loads of folk take this seriously and actually dive in like that. Like or or not, it's here and improving very very fast. Claude code (opus 4,5) or codex (5.2) would be a huge step change from what you tried, it's not even close. Be prepared when you see this, you cannot unsee it and if you mention it, you need to prepare to be lectured by folk that have not actually "really" tried it, where they will give long lectures on how silly and inexperienced you are etc. etc. Strange days, I have never lived through anything like this and seen it so close. I wonder if stagecoach drivers all had the same conversations when henry ford was putting out those cars that were slow, broke down, no seatbelts, killed folk etc. It's fascinating to watch

#🌐︱general-chat 22:48:57 traktion0_20257 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
It takes a significant effort to get it right, but even a few hours shows what these can do. I hope ... [truncated]
I suspect the tooling will only get better and break down some of the challenges too. I wanted to start with the cheaper, less capable, models and see what they could do. Certainly from the requirements/specifications I've been feeding it, the results have been perfectly good for what I was trying to do. I was expecting to need opus to get useful results, but very pleasantly surprised. It will be interesting to hit the limits of what gemini can do. So far, it feels like its issues are caused by my lack of precision, but maybe I'll expose more as usage grows. For me, I just needed to understand the reality. So many mixed messages from the industry about what gen AI can or can't do. I think seeing is believing though. I have read that small language models are showing good results too, with more specific training. I hope personal hardware will be able to run these and distribute the benefits. As you say, you can't unsee it. I think many don't want to see it. The fear is real and those who see programming as a craft are scared. As I said before, I think the negativity compounds this. Instead of doing more, with the same, the overwhelming rhetoric has been do the same, with less. So sad and I suspect it is very untrue. It feels like an opportunity to do more than we ever thought possible. Why isn't this being seen as a positive thing?

📅 Monday, 2026-01-19
#🌐︱general-chat 07:55:19 dirvine.
I think we all have to consider where this goes. Engineering, creativity, and other fields will all stay, but things have changed quite dramatically. Here is a little bit of an insight into how I work: I use many different AIs and different tools, so it's not one single process. I use many skills, plugins, and so on. It's very complex and quite personal, so there's no point in sharing it. However, now you can do things like: "My home directory is quite messy, please tidy it up," or, "My photo album has got duplicates, please remove them." Or consider this piece of code I'm working on: Does it exist anywhere else? Are there any other libraries that include this type of functionality? I also don't install code or scripts any more, AI does all that and any config changes I need on the computer too. For a function I'm working on, you could ask: 1. Has it ever been done before? 2. Check Reddit, Stack Overflow, and Hacker News to see if it has been done and if there is any feedback on it in terms of usefulness, problems, or unintended side effects. 3. Perform a parallel or competitive analysis on what I'm creating. 4. Is there anything anyone else is doing in this realm which I should be considering here? 5. Is what I'm doing unique, or does it already exist? (If it already exists, let's just use that existing thing). Plus, you can do things like: "I need this functionality, but I don't want you to download a dependency; I want you to write that functionality from scratch in this repo." We are reaching a situation where third-party dependencies might no longer be something we need. At the moment we do, but very, very soon we won't. Ultimately, it will all come back to engineering, creativity, and orchestration. Using AI for all those functions means that you have a phenomenal researcher, someone to bounce ideas off, and then a whole team of people to implement it, test it, handle DevOps, get it released, and even market it. It is not just that these things can code; writing code is a tiny, tiny element of where we are right now.

#🌐︱general-chat 15:33:08 aceweb1978 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
I would not like to be the guy who decides on what data gets uploaded and what does not and all the ... [truncated]
Ultimately, it is the foundation's money, and it is up to the Foundation to decide how to spend it for the development of the network. The Foundation has the right to make decisions about what it wants to upload to the network for public relations, for marketing, for the technological development of the network, for human history, and so on. If someone believes that "something else" should be uploaded to the network, then they are free to create their own foundation and upload the data they consider most useful for whomever. Each Foundation proceeds from its own social or technological goals and views. People who donate to a Foundation study its goals, principles, and strategies, and then these people decide whether or not to contribute their funds to a particular foundation, based on their personal preferences. Therefore, I see no problem with the Foundation's management making decisions based on the Foundation's officially announced principles and personal convictions. In the end, a broad discussion about what should be uploaded to the network for centuries to come, into history, will benefit the expansion of the network and stimulate the emergence of additional sources for data upload, and possibly other private or public Foundations. The Madesafe Foundation will demonstrate a good example of this. So, friends, dare boldly – decide what you consider necessary and important to upload to the network based on the Foundation's announced goals and personal convictions. Set an example of the Public application of the network's capabilities and Autonomi 2.0 technology!

I can see the risks there, but I'd argue that there is a lot of data which is complimentary to the network ethos and its goals. Will everyone agree with every item uploaded? Of course not, but if the reasons for choosing said items is sound, I'm sure folks would understand. Stuff like http://www.oldversion.com/, or various linux distributions, open source software in general. Perhaps scientific data, historic data, etc, etc. If things got really fractious, material could be suggested and voted on too. There could be a whole list of quality data sources, which a good majority of folk agree with. Never perfect, but transparent. This could also last resort for network safety stuff. It doesn't need to be routine, unless there is a goal in mind from doing so. Let's hope it is never needed to keep the lights on.

#🌐︱general-chat 21:51:01 janeytheterrible → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
Always a possibility.
We are also seeing the effects of the initial window size of QUINN/QUIC still being 10MB. Initial uploads of records are failing by filling up ISP grade router's buffers, and even when retrying with the auto-reduced window size sees many failures due to buffers still too full. When is the initial windows size going to be reduced from the 10MB? Its more pronounced due this time of high churn, And one expects will be the norm once people are doing downloads in a worldwide adopted network and doing heaps of downloads

📅 Wednesday, 2026-02-11
#🌐︱general-chat 10:30:00 dirvine.
It seems this age verification system is real and coming to this platform. That's absolutely unacceptable. I can't believe they're going to do that; I think the vast majority of communities on this platform will literally just leave. I don't want the usual knee-jerk reaction like, "Oh my God, privacy!" or "They're stealing things!" There's obviously something behind it, but I think if they move ahead with verification and video identification—regardless of how it's done, even if it's open source and we can see exactly what's happening—it just won't be acceptable. We really need things to move on at a much faster pace. I quite like a point Barack Obama made about this: there's no point in shouting or screaming if you're not acting, because then you're not changing anything. So, it's time for action. Of course, everybody can shout and scream and give their "amazing" in-depth opinion on why getting face-scanned is a bad thing, but it's simply not acceptable. Full stop. That's the end of the story. What we need now is action that provides a much higher level of guarantees in terms of security. Anyone who says security is "guaranteed" is either lying or just not very well-informed, but you can certainly be better secured than face-scanning people and using centralized servers. Those two elements are not acceptable. It's time for action, and that's just what we'll do.

#🌐︱general-chat 10:46:51 dirvine.
I think we have to investigate everything all at once. Autonomi 2.0 is looking like it's quite critical now. So there's a lot, but I think investigating everything would be good.Of course, being the small team that we are, we couldn't investigate everything for everybody or give our opinion on everyone's findings. But I think if this community is strong and powerful—which I believe it is—then as a community, we should be able to do it ourselves. I would just urge people: 1. Not to request permission from the MaidSafe team 2. Not to imagine that the MaidSafe team are their AI or Google and ask loads of questions I think everybody in the community can dive in, have their own opinion, and sort it out amongst ourselves. Don't shout out for the MaidSafe team because they are literally against the wall right now. They need to concentrate on acting, and we need to let them continually act. It would be good to work within the community. Don't ping me or Bux or anybody else continually wanting answers. That is always going to stop progress, and it stops the community's efforts in finding solutions. Let's just do it within the community. Let's imagine the MaidSafe team doesn't exist at the moment. How would we, as a community, get around this?

#🌐︱general-chat 17:34:24 dirvine.
There is going to have to be a change in communications between the community and us. We are going to move at a lightning pace here, which means we will dodge left and dodge right, but move forward very quickly. This also means we potentially won't have a roadmap that goes out one, two, or three years only to be held to it. We are going to move incredibly fast toward the vision we had at the start and still have right now. I think a lot of people are happy to get a roadmap that lasts for a year so they can hold people accountable: "Have you done exactly this and exactly that?" We are going to have to take a different approach here. It has to be about whether we are better this week than we were the previous week. We have to do that every week; we have to be better than the week before. We are going to move very quickly. We won't know exactly what we are doing at every step, and I don't think we'll be able to put out big, long roadmaps. That is my personal, honest opinion.

#🌐︱general-chat 17:54:14 dirvine.
I think that's something that is quite good to know. When I was not very well, it was a little bit not good, actually. I didn't let anyone visit me because I didn't want anybody saying, "I hope," or "maybe," or "possibly," or "I think it'll be okay," or anything like that. The only way I got through what I had to get through was being 100% positive: we're doing it, and we're going to pull through. I see the network the same way. I'm not interested in "maybe it'll work," "possibly it'll be okay," or "maybe this time." All I want is: this is definitely working, and there's no doubt about it. That's why I don't really interact with the community much. I don't want any negativity. Beyond that, I'm a person who can do pretty amazing things with 100% positivity. I think it's a good way to go. We're all different, and some people have to doubt (and that's fine), but we shouldn't expect that people who doubt interact with the likes of me because it would weaken me. So, in my position, it's 100% happening, and that's it. There is no question; it's just happening. This is how you make miracles happen. Doubt is how you don't.

#🌐︱general-chat 20:26:37 dirvine.
sarosa is the backbone to all of this, it's a complete rewrite with the whole vision for the network in one hand and heavily leaning into AI on the other. Whereas maidsafe started to share disk resources now we share local secure private AI and that AI can run nodes, do crypto, configure the machiens and more. Shared together via x0x (tic tc toe, the only way to not lose is to not fight) with other agents we can get groups of people sharing intelligence in ways that are more powerful that any openai / anthropic etc. So quantum secure, even more private, and fully decentralised everything, not just disk space, but all intelligence and compute etc. In addition we have true QUIC native nat traversal, quantum proof key pinning (no centralised certificates), GEO ip forced distribution (no entity can hold > 1 copy of a chunk), gossip backend and LORA/serial/bluetooth connections for when even the internet collapses. So this is the kitchen sink approach , everything !! it's the original vision but even bigger.

#🌐︱general-chat 21:07:12 dirvine.
Saorsa is a bit of a beamoth autonomi 2.0 is part of it. That's autonomi 1.0 with PQC, nat traversal, adaptive network etc. So it's autonomi 1.0 + 1, so yes nodes. However no more folk running zillions in a bunch with multiple copies of a chunk etc. No sybil, no eclipse attacks ... I could go on But the future is not folk fiddling with routers, computers and settings, it may. not even be apps, but in the short term you can run nodes like autonomi 1.0, you can have apps like 1.0 etc. (new keys though for PQC). Time is marching on, they are a changing and the network will ahve to change with it eventually. The vision was Secure Access For Everyone, now we can have that. autonomi 1.0 was secure access for techie folk autonomi 2.0 will be Secure Access For Everyone, that means you won't need to tweak, twitter about and mess with crazy configs. Fae will do all that for you, till then tweak away 😄 😄

#🌐︱general-chat 11:30:12 dirvine.
Yes, it would be a "but it could require golden binaries " type situation for sure and that locks all nodes to maidsafe supplied nodes. So it becomes a maidsafe network, which is good and bad, depends on maidsafe or what government controls maidsafe etc. I suspect the end game though is something like - You have a RULE - You need all nodes to follow that RULE - You enforce that RULE via attestation or any other meas (staking, crypto etc. etc.) - You allow anyone to build around it, but you enforce the RULE Then the RULE has to be simple and agreed on by all participants etc. etc. Long list of issues and problems one way or another though, But then life is just like this regardless, it's how much we want to trust the CE certification on our mountain climbing gear or use non certified kit and take the chance and so on. Lots of stuff and so many arguments to be had 😄 😄
👍 3
View in Discord →
#🌐︱general-chat 11:47:03 dirvine.
The key would be finding ways to decentralise the attestation keys or even better ways to enforce rules etc. What autonomi 1.0 shows us is regardless of good will, bad actors will overpower and take everything from anyone all the time. So any freedom we want for all has to mean it is not a benefit folk just get as they will suck the life ... Thing's like a Fee less native token. It won't work. Bad actors will short it, flood the network with valid spends to and forth, benefit by then buying, stop the attack and suck it dry. If folk can earn money whales will suck it all, the wee lassie in Africa making a penny to feed her family has zero chance. She can die as long as some pleb gets her cash for themselves. With freedom comes great responsibility and the screams and shouts folk will make for total freedom will not consider the cost to the poor wee lassies in Africa, cause enough folk will not care and they will make it impossible for her. These are real and valid things we must take into account. So perhaps whales cannot exist! perhaps there are things that prevent that, but give freedom to all to run nodes (autonomi 2.0) and these are the areas we need to work on, but they are quite deep really and unfortunately We have work to do to help everyone, but we need to not allow anyone to suck the life ... and they wil, even those we think of as friends and willing helpful community members, they will cause they can, they will say they are following the rules, not hacking code, but they will take everything. However good will win, I know it will

#🌐︱general-chat 12:19:31 traktion0_20257 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
The key would be finding ways to decentralise the attestation keys or even better ways to enforce ru ... [truncated]
Thanks @dirvine It is a thorny issue for sure. I think as long as anyone can provide attestation, there is scope for another entity to take over if maidsafe is put over a barrel. Like, if anyone can provide an attested binary, but folks must choose who to trust, then it seems reasonable. Normally, that would be maidsafe, but in the event of difficulties, another trusted party could issue binaries (or code/rules, etc) and node runners could choose to run them. As long as the network isn't permanently locked into a single supplier, then there are recovery options.

#🎙︱discussion-topics 20:58:46 dirvine.
autonomi 2.0 is a totally differnt network paradigm, true native nat traveersal and much much more, if you look at the IETF spec for QUIC there are tons of parameters to tweak. To test them all needs a true decentrlaised p2p network for a start and then. about a thousand years of upgrades secondly. There is testing going on, millions of tests, People tend to really shout at us to do a particular needle in a haystack tweak without - understanding the design - knowing the network - reading the specs - checking the source code Rob did some amazing detective work in autonomi 1.0, assuming it's relevant in autonomi 2.0 is a big stretch and honestly I won't really be keen on tweaking any parameter without serious research. Just saying change X cuase we heard the word X is not the way, Rob knows, as I do, networking is very complex, that parpameters are vast and there is no single setting that works for all. So I hope this does not become another native token frenzy, we need to either: - know much more or - beliive in the people who do or - Do the test ourself and show results If folk want a change they are really gonna have to fully understand what the change is and why. Cause "he said so", will never do it 😉 Not me, not Rob, nobody, we cannot have folk asking for network wide changes wihtout knowing consequences. Well we can, but they cannot expect to be taken seriously
❤️ 2
View in Discord →
#🎙︱discussion-topics 21:07:05 dirvine.
There is a libpp2 issue @Traktion but it's not that param, we did test it and it was pretty bad, we lost data and had to reverse out quickly back to what it was. I think that particular param issue was a good idea, but it did not work in practice for us as @Shu eludes to. It caused damage as nodes stopped connecting properly and some other bits. It was mixed in with other stuff, but we reversed it out and the network settled muhc more.

#🎙︱discussion-topics 23:21:15 janeytheterrible → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
autonomi 2.0 is a totally differnt network paradigm, true native nat traveersal and much much more, ... [truncated]
As long as flow control, a fundamental of any communications, is being optimised, for the home situations, from the start of the connection then all good. Otherwise these blowouts will continue to occur, like night/day. Default QUIC parameters is designed for datacentres and like stream_window_size had to be toned down to 1MB, the overall max_windows_size needs to be tamed for the home usage

#🎙︱discussion-topics 21:47:06 janeytheterrible → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
I kinda have not aimed at data centers and working well for huge corporate networks 😉 Sorta my thing ... [truncated]
Here is an exchange where testing showed they had to reduce the window size due to frame blocking to get better results. Note a frame is a window_sized data block. When set to 10MB Max (initial) then router buffers overflow with just a few chunks from the one machine (multiple nodes here) https://www.mail-archive.com/quic%40ietf.org/msg03789.html And here, while only one QUIC on a machine (we have one for each node, so multiply the issues and therein is the problems of router buffer overflow) https://docs.google.com/document/d/1F2YfdDXKpy20WVKJueEf4abn_LVZHhMUMS5gX6Pgjl4/mobilebasic Even Google sets their default initial window sizes to very small values ``` Default values Google’s servers default to enable auto-tuning for receive buffers, with the following initial window size settings: CFCW: 49152 // 48 KB SFCW: 32768 // 32 KB ``` And the QUIC documents says to set that value for your application. Remember with multiple nodes (independent QUICs) then buffer size in the potato ISP routers are a constraint that QUIC QUIC does not know about and has to learn for each new connection (chunk/node) ``` There is a maximum flow control receive window size which is chosen independently by each endpoint. An endpoint should base this decision on available resources - if you are not constrained by memory then set a large window of 100 Mb for example, whereas a busy server may set a window of 64 Kb. ```

#🎙︱discussion-topics 15:00:10 dirvine.
What we do now so folk know. setup several computers all over the world and in local networks. Run them all with AI, full logging on (asyn) Have the AI monitor logs, look for inconsistencies Do deep research on each finding, involve many AI's in deep research max thinking mode Work with them, agree a plan Run test again, both tests fully benchmarked. Not notions or idea or suggestions, real world testing of ALL of the tweaks we can make It is super super easy to convince ourselves of a single parameter like this discussion and just doggedly go after that one param, never to ask why? It may be correct @neo it may well be, but it's one thing of thousands of other things that all must be considered together. Otherwise there would be a - Data center - Home user Setting and we would be done with it. It's seriously not simple and it's absolutely 100% certain not a single param change we need to consider.

#🎙︱discussion-topics 06:25:02 dimitarsafenetworkbulgaria → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
There was some point when I was ill that we changed from a community that could collaborate on an eq ... [truncated]
This is normal and expected behavior. We have a group of people who want to help, but because they don't have a complete picture of the whole picture, they think that the color of the paint determines how stable the ship is, and they really want the color to be just right so that the ship can be stable. No matter how much the engineer explains that color doesn't affect stability, people feel that it does because color is what they see. When it comes to such feelings, it's a waste of time to discuss them, but to keep the community strong, it's good to pay attention to feelings. For this purpose, I have an AI persona that I use to talk acknowledging people's feelings and at the same time not wasting my time.

#🎙︱discussion-topics 10:02:00 dirvine.
To me, we need to get to a position where nodes are adaptive and can actually self-configure. There is no real "golden set" of configurations; networking is a nightmare, which is why there are so many settings, yet none of them suit everybody. While advanced users in the community might not mind tweaking things, that isn't the goal here. The goal should be "click, install, and work," so nodes must become much more adaptive. Where we do have issues—which QUIC tries to address—is when you need negotiated protocol settings. There are quite a lot of them, and that's another angle to consider. It is a bit like a spaceship: if someone suggests taking one part out, it feels dangerous. I believe the network will eventually evolve into an adaptive mechanism where nodes self-configure over time. That is the ultimate goal, because otherwise, networking is simply too hard. People will always want the specific configuration they think suits them, but the amount of parameters is staggering. Since personal networks change constantly, leaning toward adaptive nodes is a key component.
👍 4
View in Discord →
#🎙︱discussion-topics 10:12:56 dirvine.
I feel we should move away from this "node runner" concept, which is probably alarming for some people. The key really is that these nodes should run on everyone's computer without needing lots of configuration, and they certainly should not be affecting the computer or the network adversely. Basically, they should be an almost invisible component of the applications people are running. That invisible component should allow them to share the resources of their machine with people who have fewer resources, who may need to use tokens to store data instead of buying new machines. The winning component is that even if your machine blows up or catches fire, your data is still okay if you are storing it on the network. I feel like we are missing the target with the amount of work being done for node runners. When it becomes a "node runner" conversation, it all becomes about money: 1. How much am I getting? 2. How much can I earn? 3. How many of these things can I get to make more money? While that is fascinating in some ways, I think it means we've missed the target slightly. It should be about secure access for everyone. Everyone should be able to participate fully in the network, and the "node runner" thing has just never felt very good to me. It has been great to test nodes, but that is not the direction. The direction is that these nodes should be really beneficial to everybody. People should have a clear understanding that their data is secure—not only from people reading it, but from the raw hardware melting down.
👍 5
View in Discord →
#🎙︱discussion-topics 10:41:49 27poke → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
I feel we should move away from this "node runner" concept, which is probably alarming for some peop ... [truncated]
From my own experience people don't like to have their computers on 24/7 and nodes tend to love a life were they are always on and don't cause churn across the network.
👍 2
View in Discord →
#🎙︱discussion-topics 10:44:56 dirvine.
This is another advantage of AI. We're moving into a different world where these things will be on 24/7. But even without it being on 24/7, if the network is adaptive enough, that shouldn't matter. Autonomi 2, for instance, has a neural network and LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory), which allows it to: 1. Predict when particular computers are off and on 2. Work out churn prediction This is what I mean about being adaptive: the network has to be adaptive, and not only in the networking core, if you know what I mean.

#🎙︱discussion-topics 14:07:48 traktion0_20257 → replied to dirvine.
dirvine. said:
I feel we should move away from this "node runner" concept, which is probably alarming for some peop ... [truncated]
Personally, I think all flavours of node running should be tolerated. I suspect there will always be a mix of flavours too, unless there is code to be hostile against certain uses (which may be tough in itself). For example, I may want a 'light' app which doesn't do node duties. A simple, thin client app. Likewise, I may want a 'heavy' app, which runs a node too, as it is long running and I don't want to think about node setup. I may want to run the 'light' version on my phone, but then run some nodes on spare, old, hardware at home to earn tokens to fund the 'light' app operation. So, it feels like a choice thing. Maybe the default should be the simplest, but power users will always tinker. There may also be a question of long running nodes, which need 24/7 access, to avoid heavy churn, improve network performance, etc. It feels like there nedes to be a balance across these different needs of users and the network.

#🎙︱discussion-topics 15:33:09 dirvine.
Without a doubt, all you say is 100% agreed in my head. What I am saying is not to prevent any use, but to shift focus from power users (node runners) to ordinary people. It's code, folk can and will do what they want, but we should not target power users and instead make it super super simple for ordinary people to be part of the eco-system. If you look on here or the community forum there is so much, how many nodes can I run, how much do I get and all that kind of thing. While it's superb folk run nodes to help (most of the old community do just that), the community that is building has an ever growing presence of folk who want to earn, ignore the wee lassie in Africa, just show me the money. So the shift from community for the good can shift to community for the money, bitcoin did that and we are heading that way. So the focus, the true grit of this has to be value for the wider audience. When true decentralisation and value creation and flow becomes an obvious incentive to simply extract cash, that is what will happen and we risk that very thing. Ultimately the good folk don't see it, they cannot notice it, but this is what I feel we are heading towards. So absolutely there wil always be all ways of using this, but if we continue to focus on node runners and token extraction alone we failed. We must provide value for all, then the ANT token value rises and we all win. Not a select few who managed to grab theirs and run.

I hope you realize that I’m saying this from a place of respect as well and wouldn’t say it if I didn’t have the networks best interest at heart. I do think that sometimes you can lash out a bit at people because they ask something for the first, maybe second time, because you’ve heard the question/remark/advice a thousand times already. And while I understand from your point of view that that 1000th question justifies pushing back, but I think you’re pushing back at the wrong people.
